AST-65.measurement_goal: Measure coverage and currency of the ISMS scope statement: percent of identified information assets, business processes, third‑party interfaces and geographic locations included in the formal ISMS scope; frequency and timeliness of documented scope reviews and the count of scope changes with stakeholder sign‑off.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is primarily a preventive governance control that verifies coverage (percentage of assets/processes/locations included) and currency (percent compliant with review cadence). Counts (review events, changes) and trends (reviews over time) are useful secondary measures to show ongoing maintenance.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Report the proportion of canonical inventory items (information assets, business processes, third‑party interfaces, geographic sites) that are explicitly tagged as 'in‑scope' in the ISMS scope statement; count of scope review events and scope-change records with timestamps and approver identities over the last 12 months; and presence of stakeholder consultation records and signed scope approvals.
data_signals: ISMS scope statement document (version history, effective date, author/approver)
asset inventory records with scope_tag or scope_status field
business process register entries mapped to scope_flag
third‑party/service interface registry entries mapped to scope
scope review meeting minutes and stakeholder consultation attendance/approval records
scope change log entries (timestamp, change author, justification)
audit/assessment findings referencing scope mismatches
frequency/timestamps of scheduled scope review events
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: formal versioned scope statement with time‑stamped approvals and change log entries, automated asset-inventory tags showing full mapping to scope, and regular scheduled review events documented over multiple periods.
Weak: single static scope document without version history or approvals, manual one-time mapping spreadsheets, or anecdotal stakeholder statements without recorded sign-off.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control directs definition and maintenance of ISMS boundaries and scope (planning and documentation) to prevent omissions and misalignment, indicating a preventive governance control.

AST-65.differentiation_note: This control specifically addresses scope definition, coverage and review cadence for the ISMS (documentation, stakeholder sign‑off, mapping of assets/processes/third parties), rather than operational asset handling, technical controls, or training activities; measurements therefore focus on coverage percentages, versioned documents, and review frequency.

AST-66.measurement_goal: Measure the coverage and accuracy of the asset inventory: percentage of all information assets recorded in the inventory (by type and criticality), percentage of inventory entries with an assigned owner, frequency of inventory updates, and count of discrepancies found and resolved in audit cycles.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This control is primarily about coverage and completeness (coverage % of assets inventoried and % with owners), with supporting absolute counts (unowned or missing assets) and trends over time to show maintenance and improvement.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Compute the percentage of discovered/known assets recorded in the canonical asset register (CMDB) split by asset type (data, hardware, software) and criticality; count of asset records missing owner or classification; timestamped inventory update frequency per asset; number and resolution status of audit discrepancies with timestamps.
data_signals: asset registry entries with asset_id, asset_type (data/hardware/software), classification, criticality, owner_id, and last_update_timestamp
automated discovery/scan outputs or sync logs showing assets discovered vs persisted in CMDB (discovery_timestamp, sync_status, error_reason)
count of inventory records lacking owner_id or classification tag
audit discrepancy records linking inventory_id to discrepancy_type, discovered_timestamp, remediation_timestamp, and resolution_status
asset lifecycle status fields (active, deployed, retired, disposed) and disposal/return records (e.g., employee termination hardware return logs)
frequency of inventory updates per asset (update_event timestamps) and scheduled review logs
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: continuous automated discovery logs and successful CMDB sync records with recent last_update_timestamps and assigned owner_id for >95% of assets; audit discrepancy records showing timely remediation.
Weak: point-in-time spreadsheets, manual one-off inventories, long gaps since last_update_timestamp, high proportion of records without owner or classification, or audit findings unresolved.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control defines a comprehensive register, ownership assignment and ongoing maintenance to prevent asset mismanagement and enable proper protection, which are preventive activities.

AST-66.differentiation_note: This control focuses specifically on inventory coverage, ownership assignment and update frequency (coverage/completeness metrics) rather than detection, remediation timing, or configuration hardening used in sibling controls.

AST-67.measurement_goal: Measure the completeness and timeliness of asset returns during employee/contractor offboarding by reporting the percentage of assigned assets returned on schedule, the count and types of outstanding assets after termination, and incidents of unauthorized access or data exposure linked to retained assets.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: The control is preventive and about coverage/compliance (percent of assets returned on time). Counts of outstanding assets and trends in return rates provide complementary evidence of operational performance and improvement over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: For each offboarding event, capture asset assignments and returns with timestamps and asset identifiers, the offboarding checklist completion status, and any related incidents. Build metrics: percent of assets returned within the policy timeline per termination, count of outstanding assets by type and days overdue, and count of incidents attributable to retained assets.
data_signals: asset inventory records with assigned owner and asset serial/ID
asset assignment timestamp (date issued) and asset return timestamp (date returned)
offboarding checklist completion records (signed/automated) with timestamps
exit interview records noting asset obligations
automated asset-tracking system logs showing custody changes
count of outstanding/unreturned assets X days after termination
incident reports or breach records referencing retained assets or unauthorized access post-termination
asset disposal/decommission receipts or return receipts
policy / SLA for asset return timelines
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated asset-management logs showing assignment and return timestamps, signed/recorded return receipts, and reconciliation within policy SLA for each termination.
Strong: linkage of incident records to specific retained asset IDs to show impact.
Weak: manual one-time spreadsheets or undocumented verbal confirmations without timestamps.
Weak: annual spot-checks or infrequent audits that do not show per-offboarding compliance or overdue counts.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control requires policies and processes to ensure assets are returned on termination to prevent unauthorized access (e.g., 'establish a formal asset return policy', 'ensure assets are returned').

AST-67.differentiation_note: This control specifically focuses on offboarding and physical/logical asset return measurement (timeliness, outstanding counts, and related incidents), unlike sibling asset controls that might focus on identification, classification, or protection of assets during their operational lifecycle.

AST-68.measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of identified IP assets that are inventoried and classified, the percentage of IP-tagged assets with enforced access controls, the count and trend of reported IP breaches/violations per period, and the training completion and awareness score for employees responsible for IP handling.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is a preventive control focused on coverage and compliance (e.g., percent of IP assets inventoried, percent of employees completing IP training). Percentages show coverage/compliance; counts and trends show event volumes and changes over time (reported breaches, audits performed).

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: 1) Percentage of intellectual property assets present in the centralized IP inventory (asset id, owner, classification) versus expected IP asset list. 2) Percentage of IP-tagged assets with role-based access controls applied and recent access-review attestations. 3) Count of reported IP breaches/violations and time-to-containment/remediation per incident. 4) Number and frequency of IP policy audits and the employee IP training completion rate and awareness survey scores.
data_signals: IP asset inventory records (asset_id, classification, owner, creation/update timestamps)
access control lists / role assignments for IP-tagged resources (resource_id, principal, permission, last_reviewed)
audit records of IP management policy reviews (audit_id, date, scope, findings)
incident/violation reports labeled 'IP breach' or 'IP violation' (incident_id, detection_time, resolution_time, root_cause)
employee training records for IP policy (user_id, course_id, completion_timestamp, score)
license and IP agreement registry with compliance status (agreement_id, effective_date, compliance_flag)
asset return / termination logs (employee_id, returned_asset_id, return_date) relevant to IP-containing devices
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: continuous-maintained IP inventory reconciled to configuration management and access control systems, automated access logs and periodic automated audits with timestamps.
Strong: training completion with assessment scores and periodic awareness survey trend data.
Weak: one-time manual attestations, annual-only penetration tests, or undocumented verbal confirmations.
Weak: incident counts without root-cause or resolution timestamps that prevent measuring remediation effectiveness.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The description emphasizes safeguarding assets via policy, classification, access controls and training — all preventive measures to stop unauthorized use or disclosure.

AST-68.differentiation_note: Unlike general asset or incident controls, this control specifically requires measurement of IP-specific inventory/classification, license agreement compliance, and IP-focused training/awareness rather than generic asset configuration or vulnerability remediation metrics.

AST-69.measurement_goal: Measure percent of organizational assets used or stored off‑premises that are inventoried and compliant with encryption and access‑control policies, the count and rate of security incidents involving off‑premises assets, and the completion rate of employee training on off‑site asset handling.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This preventive control requires evidence of coverage and compliance (best shown as percentages: percent of off‑prem assets inventoried, percent encrypted, percent of employees trained). Counts and rates are useful secondary metrics for incidents and asset return events; trends show improvement or degradation over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: 1) Percentage of off‑prem assets (by type: laptop, mobile, removable media, cloud storage) present in the canonical asset inventory and flagged as 'off‑premises'. 2) Percentage of those assets with encryption enabled (at rest and/or in transit) and with enforced access controls (MDM enrollment, ACLs). 3) Count and rate of security incidents tied to off‑prem assets and percent of terminated employees for whom asset return was completed. 4) Employee training completion rate for off‑prem asset handling.
data_signals: off‑premises asset inventory records with asset type, owner, location flag
encryption status per asset (at‑rest and in‑transit flags / encryption enforcement logs)
MDM/endpoint management enrollment and policy compliance status
access control lists / privileged access assignments for off‑prem assets
transportation/transfer logs showing secure transfer method (SFTP/courier receipts / encrypted transfer timestamps)
LMS training completion records for 'asset security' or 'off‑site asset handling' including timestamps and user IDs
incident/IR tickets tagged as 'off‑premises asset' with timestamps and severity
asset return/termination checklists and signed return receipts
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated asset inventory (MDM/CMDB) with real‑time encryption and compliance flags is strong evidence; manual spreadsheets or one‑off attestations are weak.
Endpoint management or cloud provider logs proving encryption in transit/at rest and enforced access controls are strong; device owner self‑reports are weak.
LMS records with completion timestamps and assessments are strong evidence of training coverage; informal awareness communications without completion records are weak.
Incident logs that explicitly link events to off‑prem assets and include remediation timelines are strong; generic incident counts without asset linkage are weak.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The description emphasizes establishing policies, procedures, encryption, secure transport and access controls to prevent breaches of off‑premises assets.

AST-69.differentiation_note: This control specifically targets assets located or used off‑premises (physical transport, removable media, cloud storage and associated employee behavior/training), requiring measurements of inventory/coverage and encryption/access enforcement rather than general on‑prem asset identification.

BCD-68.measurement_goal: Measure ICT continuity readiness by tracking coverage of documented BCP/BIA artifacts for critical ICT assets (including RTO/RPO), frequency and execution success of BCP tests/drills, actual recovery times observed in drills vs. defined RTOs, and percentage of relevant staff with up-to-date continuity training and contact information.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This preventive control is best evidenced by coverage and compliance percentages (e.g., percent of critical ICT assets with documented RTO/RPO and up-to-date BCPs). Counts of tests/drills and trending of recovery time improvements provide complementary operational context.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: 1) Percentage of critical ICT assets with an associated BIA entry and documented BCP including RTO and RPO. 2) Count and timestamped records of BCP tests/drills per period, plus measured recovery time achieved per test for each critical system. 3) Percentage of required staff with documented continuity training and last-updated contact information.
data_signals: business impact analysis (BIA) records with asset criticality tags
business continuity plan (BCP) documents and version timestamps
registered RTO and RPO values per ICT asset or service
BCP test/drill execution logs with start/end timestamps
measured recovery time (actual downtime) per drill by asset
BCP test outcomes and deviation/failure logs
staff continuity training completion records with dates
contact list registry with last-updated timestamps for key personnel and external partners
ICT asset inventory mapping dependencies to business functions
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated drill execution logs with measured recovery times and pass/fail outcomes, up-to-date BIA/BCP artifacts linked to asset inventory, and timestamped training completions and contact list updates.
Weak: single annual narrative exercise report or undated policy documents without measurable recovery time data, and manually maintained spreadsheets lacking timestamps.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The description explicitly states this is a "preventive control" and focuses on preparedness activities (BIA, BCP, RTO/RPO, testing and training) to avoid or limit disruptions.

BCD-68.differentiation_note: This control specifically emphasizes preparedness, documented RTO/RPO and tested recovery times for ICT assets (readiness and training) rather than incident detection, data backup integrity, or post-incident remediation metrics.

BCD-69.measurement_goal: Measure the coverage and success of backup operations for defined critical data and systems (percentage of assets covered and backup success rate), and measure restore capability (restore test pass rate and measured RTO/RPO compliance) against defined RPO/RTO SLAs.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: The control requires demonstrating coverage and compliance with defined RPO/RTO and policy (coverage % and SLA adherence). Backup success/failure and restore events are best represented as rates/counts and trended over time to show ongoing effectiveness.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count backup job executions and outcomes per asset (success/failure) and map those to the critical asset inventory; measure the percentage of critical assets with at least one recent successful backup meeting their RPO, the restore-test pass rate, and measured restore durations compared to RTO targets. Include metadata showing storage location (on-site/off-site/cloud), encryption/versioning flags, and timestamps to prove recoverability and integrity.
data_signals: backup job execution timestamps (per asset/system)
backup job status and error codes (success | failure | partial)
measured restore attempt timestamps and restore duration (time-to-restore)
RPO and RTO values defined per data category (SLA targets)
backup storage location metadata (on-site | off-site | cloud) and retention period
backup encryption/versioning metadata (encryption enabled flag, snapshot/version id)
mapping of backup asset identifiers to production asset inventory and criticality tier
scheduled backup test/drill records and test outcomes (pass/fail) with documentation
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: continuous automated backup logs with timestamped success/failure results, linked asset inventory, and regular documented restore tests showing measured restore durations within RTO.
Strong: backup metadata that shows encryption and off-site storage, plus versioning and retention policy records.
Weak: single point-in-time manual backup receipts, annual-only restore tests, or lack of mapping between backups and critical asset inventory.
Weak: logs without encryption/retention metadata or without measured restore durations to prove RTO compliance.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control focuses on establishing backups, RPO/RTO, secure storage and preventive measures to ensure availability (phrases: 'creating and maintaining backup copies', 'RPO and RTO', 'frequency of backups').

BCD-69.differentiation_note: This control specifically requires measurement of backup coverage, backup/restore success and RPO/RTO compliance (creation and restoration testing), distinguishing it from broader continuity or incident response controls that focus on coordination or notification rather than backup technical metrics.

BCD-70.measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of critical information systems that have independently provisioned redundant processing facilities with geographic diversity, the failover test pass rate, and operational recovery performance (MTTR and availability %) against defined SLAs.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is a preventive control focused on coverage and compliance (e.g., percent of critical systems with redundant facilities and SLA-based availability), so percentage best shows coverage; counts of redundant facilities and failover events plus trending of MTTR provide supporting context over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: 1) Percentage of critical assets with documented, independent redundant processing facilities (by asset ID and criticality tier). 2) Failover test pass rate and last test timestamps per asset/facility. 3) Operational recovery metrics: mean time to recovery (MTTR), failover switch-over durations, and system availability percentage over the reporting period. 4) Data replication health: replication lag and last successful replication timestamp for replicated datasets.
data_signals: inventory of critical information processing facilities with asset IDs and criticality classification
mapping of primary and redundant facility identifiers and geographic location tags
failover test execution logs (timestamp, asset_id, test result, duration)
system availability uptime metrics per facility (availability percentage, downtime windows)
mean time to recovery (MTTR) records and incident recovery timestamps
data replication status and replication lag timestamps per dataset
failover switch-over event logs (start/end times, automated/manual, outcome)
architecture diagram/versioned redundancy procedure documents with last updated timestamp
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated, timestamped failover test logs showing successful switchovers and measured durations, continuous availability telemetry and replication health metrics, and up-to-date documented architecture and procedures.
Weak: one-off manual reports or architectural diagrams without test results, or anecdotal descriptions of redundancy without replication/availability telemetry.
Strong: consistent pass rate across repeated scheduled tests and MTTR consistently within SLA; Weak: single successful test with no follow-up or missing replication timestamps.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The description calls it a "preventive control designed to ensure the continuous availability" and specifies redundant systems, geographic diversity and replication to prevent failures.

BCD-70.differentiation_note: This control specifically requires measurement of redundant facility coverage, geographic diversity and failover test effectiveness (MTTR and replication lag) rather than general continuity planning or incident handling metrics.

CFG-44.measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of assets with an approved baseline configuration applied and continuously monitored, the count and rate of detected unauthorized configuration changes, and the mean time to remediate configuration deviations and misconfigurations; also measure frequency and coverage of configuration audits against the documented configuration management policy.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: The control is preventive and emphasizes coverage and baseline compliance (best expressed as percentages of assets in-compliance). Counts of unauthorized changes and trending of remediation times are important secondary measures to demonstrate effectiveness over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: 1) Percentage of total assets (by environment and criticality) that have the approved baseline configuration deployed and verified by automated scans; 2) count of unauthorized or undocumented configuration changes detected per reporting period and mean time-to-remediate per incident; 3) audit frequency and percent coverage of assets reviewed versus inventory. Group these by asset type, environment (prod/non-prod), and criticality so queries can filter and aggregate accordingly.
data_signals: asset inventory records (asset_id, asset_type, owner, environment, criticality)
baseline configuration version per asset (baseline_id, version, approval_timestamp)
configuration scan execution logs (timestamp, scanner_id, asset_id, baseline_match_flag, drift_details)
configuration drift/change detection alerts (alert_id, timestamp, asset_id, diff_summary, detected_by_tool)
change request and approval records (CR_id, submitter, approver, approval_timestamp, related_asset_id)
unauthorized change incidents (incident_id, asset_id, timestamp, change_description, detected_by, detection_timestamp)
remediation tickets and timestamps (ticket_id, asset_id, remediation_start_time, remediation_end_time, status)
configuration audit reports (audit_id, scope, execution_date, findings_count, assets_covered_count)
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: continuous automated configuration scan logs and drift alerts tied to asset inventory and baseline versions plus accompanying change request/approval records.
Strong: demonstrable remediation timestamps (start and completion) for each detected deviation and consistent audit reports with coverage metrics.
Weak: point-in-time manual spreadsheets or one-off audit reports without automated scan logs or missing linkage to change approvals.
Weak: incomplete asset inventory or tool coverage (agents not deployed) which prevents verification of baseline deployment.

affinity_keywords: time taken to remediate configuration issues

control_type_classification.reasoning: The description explicitly states 'Configuration management is a critical preventive control' and focuses on establishing baselines, documenting configurations, and preventing unauthorized changes.

CFG-44.differentiation_note: This control specifically focuses on baseline configuration deployment, continuous tracking, and preventive coverage (percentage of assets compliant) rather than purely on change execution or incident response—measurement must emphasize baseline compliance and drift detection tied to asset inventory and approvals.

CHG-37.measurement_goal: Measure the proportion of changes that follow the formal change process (have impact assessments, documented approvals, and rollback plans), track change implementation time and the count/rate of incidents attributable to changes so auditors can verify changes are planned, authorized, and do not introduce security or operational impact.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This control is preventive and emphasizes coverage/compliance (e.g., percent of changes with impact assessments/approvals/rollback plans). Secondary counts and rates (change volume, change-related incidents, implementation time) provide complementary event-level evidence and trends over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Calculate: (a) percentage of change requests with an attached documented impact assessment, approvals recorded, and a rollback plan; (b) count and rate of change-related incidents linked to change IDs; and (c) implementation duration per change (implementation_end - implementation_start). Measurements should be joinable by change_id and filterable by change type and criticality.
data_signals: change request records (change_id, submitter, change_type, criticality)
impact assessment document or impact_assessment_present flag and timestamp
approval records (approver_id, approval_timestamp, approval_decision) linked to change_id
change log entries with state transitions and timestamps (submitted/approved/implemented/closed)
rollback plan attachment or rollback_plan_present flag linked to change_id
implementation start and end timestamps to compute time_to_implement
post-change incident records referencing change_id (incident_id, incident_timestamp, incident_type)
unauthorized or emergency change flags (changes implemented without approvals)
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated change management system records with immutable timestamps, approver identities, attached impact assessments and rollback plans, and explicit linkage from incidents to change_id.
Moderate: centralized change log exported from change tool but with manual attachments or inconsistent approval metadata.
Weak: ad-hoc spreadsheets or email trails lacking consistent change_id, no timestamps, or no linkage between changes and incidents.

affinity_keywords: frequency of incidents related to changes

control_type_classification.reasoning: The description focuses on planning, approvals, impact assessments and rollback planning to prevent disruptions and security vulnerabilities, which are preventive activities.

CHG-37.differentiation_note: This control specifically requires measurement of planning and authorization artifacts (impact assessments, approvals, rollback plans and their coverage) rather than post-implementation detection or remediation metrics; its unique need is verifying process compliance and coverage for every change.

CHG-38.measurement_goal: Measure the proportion of software installation events on operational systems that were pre-approved and vetted, the count and rate of unauthorized installations detected, and the timeliness of approval processing (time-to-approve) and enforcement on endpoints.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: The control requires demonstrating coverage/compliance (e.g., 'only approved and vetted applications are deployed'), so a percentage of installations that were approved is primary; counts and rates (unauthorized installs, approvals) and trends over time supplement coverage metrics to show detection and remediation velocity.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count installation events per host with attributes (package name/version, host id, user/service account, timestamp) and link each event to an approval record (approval_id, approver, approval_timestamp). Compute: 1) percent of installation events with prior approval, 2) count and rate of installation events without approval (unauthorized), 3) mean and distribution of time_between(approval_request_timestamp, approval_decision_timestamp), and 4) inventory drift: installed_items not present in approved_software_inventory per host.
data_signals: installation logs with package name, version, host ID, installer identity and timestamp
software approval workflow records (approval_id, request_timestamp, decision, approver, approval_timestamp)
approved software inventory (whitelist) with versions and permitted hosts/environment
endpoint/MDM enforcement events (policy block/allow, enforcement failures)
periodic software inventory/audit reports (scans showing installed applications per host)
unauthorized installation detection alerts and remediation tickets
time_to_approve metrics derived from approval request and approval timestamps
audit trail linking deployment tool logs (CI/CD, software distribution) to approval records
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: continuous, automated linkage between installation events and prior approval records (installation event contains approval_id) and endpoint enforcement blocking unauthorized installs.
Strong: comprehensive approved_software_inventory and automated periodic software inventory scans showing low drift.
Weak: occasional manual audit reports or screenshots without machine-readable installation logs or missing timestamps linking to approval workflow.
Weak: only post-incident detection without records of pre-approval or enforcement logs.

affinity_keywords: time taken to approve new software requests

control_type_classification.reasoning: The description mandates review and authorization before installation ('all software must be reviewed and authorized before installation'), which is a preventive requirement.

CHG-38.differentiation_note: This control specifically targets the pre-approval, inventory and enforcement of software installations on operational systems (measuring pre-approval linkage and enforcement) rather than broader change management activities like emergency change procedures or post-deployment testing.

CLD-7.measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of cloud services covered by approved cloud security policy controls (risk assessment, contractual security requirements, encryption-in-transit/at-rest, defined roles, and exit strategy) and the count and resolution rate of cloud-related security incidents and provider audit findings over time.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: The control emphasizes coverage and policy compliance across the cloud service lifecycle (best expressed as percentages of services in compliance). Incident tracking and remediation velocity are appropriately captured as counts/rates and trends.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: For each cloud service record: (1) presence of completed risk assessment and approved cloud security policy mapping; (2) contract or SLA containing required security clauses; (3) encryption enabled for data in transit and at rest; (4) assigned cloud roles/responsibilities and segregation-of-duties checks; (5) documented exit/termination strategy and recent provider audit/status. Also measure cloud-related incident counts and remediation times.
data_signals: cloud service inventory with service identifier and criticality
risk assessment records per cloud service (timestamp, assessor, findings)
contractual security clause flags (e.g., data handling, encryption, audit rights) per provider
configuration state: encryption-in-transit enabled flag and encryption-at-rest enabled flag
existence of documented exit/termination plan and date last reviewed
role and responsibility assignments for cloud management (owners, approvers) and segregation-of-duties exceptions
cloud-related incident logs tagged by cloud_service_id (incident timestamp, severity, root cause, remediation timestamp)
provider audit evidence and attestation documents (SOC2/ISO reports, audit dates, findings)
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated cloud inventory joined to config management showing encryption flags, automated policy compliance scans, and machine-readable contracts/SLA registry with audit evidence; incident logs with timestamps and remediation records.
Weak: single manual spreadsheets, one-off vendor emails, screenshots of settings, or annual reports without ongoing configuration or incident data.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control prescribes policies, provider selection criteria, encryption requirements and exit strategies — all preventive governance and configuration controls, with some detective elements (incidents/audits).

CLD-7.differentiation_note: This control specifically targets governance across the cloud service lifecycle (procurement to termination), so measurements must include contractual and exit-plan evidence and provider audit integration — unlike sibling controls that might focus only on detection or host configuration.

CPL-41.measurement_goal: Measure audit program execution and effectiveness by reporting: (1) count of planned vs. completed audits and the percentage completed on schedule; (2) count and severity distribution of non-conformities identified; and (3) mean and distribution of time-to-resolve corrective actions versus defined SLA/planned intervals.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: The control is detective in nature and auditors need raw counts (audits executed, non-conformities, corrective actions) to demonstrate detection; percentages (on-schedule completion) and trends (remediation time improving over time) are useful secondary measures to show program effectiveness.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of planned audit engagements (with planned dates) and corresponding actual execution timestamps; audit report records with non-conformity entries and severity tags; corrective action / remediation tickets with assigned owner, open and close timestamps and SLA targets; evidence of auditor independence (audit team membership separate from ISMS management) and audit scope mapping to ISMS components or assets.
data_signals: audit_schedule records (planned_date, audit_id, scope, objective, criteria)
audit_execution timestamps (actual_start_date, actual_end_date, auditor_id, report_id)
audit_report metadata and non-conformity records (finding_id, severity, description)
corrective_action tickets linked to findings (ticket_id, assigned_to, open_timestamp, close_timestamp, closure_evidence)
SLA or planned interval definitions for audits and remediation (SLA_by_severity, planned_interval_annual/bi-annual)
auditor assignment and independence flag (auditor_role, department, independent_from_ISMS_management)
automation/tool logs from ISMS.online (audit_automation_events, document_uploads, evidence_trail)
audit_coverage matrix mapping scope to ISMS processes/assets (coverage_percentage_by_control)
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated audit execution logs and timestamped audit reports with structured non-conformity records linked to corrective-action tickets and closure evidence.
Strong: explicit SLA or planned-interval definitions used to calculate on-schedule % and MTTR.
Weak: single point-in-time attestations or unsigned PDF reports without timestamps, absent linkage between findings and remediation tickets.
Weak: manual spreadsheets without auditor independence metadata or missing scope mapping.

affinity_keywords: percentage of audits completed on schedule

control_type_classification.reasoning: The description states the control 'serves to systematically evaluate' and 'identify gaps', which are detective activities that find non-conformities and weaknesses.

CPL-41.differentiation_note: This control focuses on program-level audit execution, finding tracking, and remediation SLA adherence (audit counts, non-conformity counts, MTTR and auditor independence) rather than technical controls or operational detection — measurement must include audit schedule, independence metadata, and linkage between findings and corrective actions.

CPL-42.measurement_goal: Measure audit plan execution (number of audits conducted vs planned), coverage of audits across critical processes/assets, the count and severity distribution of audit findings, and the percentage of audit findings remediated within defined timeframes (SLA) with trend analysis of non-conformities over time.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Audits and findings are event-based and best evidenced by counts (audits executed, findings opened/closed). Percentages and trends are useful secondary metrics to show remediation rates and improvement over time, while rates (e.g., findings closed per period) measure throughput.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of planned vs executed audits per reporting period (grouped by process/asset criticality), audit execution timestamps, audit scope and criteria mapped to organizational processes, full listing of audit findings with severity, owner, open_date and close_date, remediation SLA per severity and actual remediation timestamps, repeat-finding linkage to prior audit IDs, and auditor qualification/independence records.
data_signals: audit_plan entries (audit_id, planned_date, scope, frequency, assigned_auditor)
audit_execution records (audit_id, actual_start_date, actual_end_date, methods_used)
audit_report artifacts (report_id, findings_count, findings_by_severity, recommendations)
audit_finding records (finding_id, severity, description, open_date, due_date, close_date, remediation_owner, status)
remediation SLA table or policy (SLA_by_severity)
repeat_finding flags referencing previous_audit_id or previous_finding_id
auditor qualifications and independence declarations (auditor_id, certifications, external/internal indicator)
audit_report distribution / acknowledgement logs (who received report, timestamp)
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: structured audit_execution records and automated audit_plan vs actual dashboards with timestamps, finding records with clear remediation timestamps and linkage to evidence (patch IDs, configuration changes), and documented auditor independence.
Weak: isolated PDF audit reports or one-off summary slides without machine-readable finding records, missing open/close timestamps, or lack of documented auditor qualifications.
Strong: repeat-finding linkage showing decline in recurrence over multiple audit cycles; Weak: ad-hoc notes of remediations without evidence of sustained closure or verification.

affinity_keywords: percentage of audit findings addressed within a specified timeframe

control_type_classification.reasoning: The description emphasizes assessing effectiveness, identifying weaknesses and verifying adherence — activities that detect non-conformities and gaps (audit findings).

CPL-42.differentiation_note: This control is governance-focused and requires measuring plan vs execution, auditor independence, findings lifecycle and trend of non-conformities — unlike operational controls it emphasizes audit coverage, remediation SLA compliance and repeat-finding analysis rather than technical control performance metrics.

CPL-43.measurement_goal: Measure the volume and lifecycle of nonconformities (count created), the time-to-resolution and closure rate of corrective actions, the percent of nonconformities with completed root cause analyses and verified follow-up assessments, and the recurrence rate of similar nonconformities over time.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: As a corrective-control the most useful primary metric is a rate (e.g., closure rate, remediation verification rate, recurrence rate) to show remediation activity; counts (number of nonconformities), percentages (RCA completion %, verification success %) and trends over time are complementary to demonstrate sustained effectiveness.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of nonconformity records created per period and their lifecycle timestamps; time from nonconformity detection to corrective action implementation and to verification/closure; presence of a completed root cause analysis document and a follow-up assessment for each nonconformity; linkage or tagging to prior similar nonconformities to compute recurrence rate; timestamps and records showing policy/procedure or training updates resulting from corrective actions.
data_signals: nonconformity report ID and creation timestamp
assigned owner/responsible party for corrective action
corrective action plan record with due date and implementation timestamp
root cause analysis completed flag and RCA document link
verification / follow-up assessment result and timestamp
nonconformity closure / resolution timestamp
policy/procedure change log entries and training update records with timestamps
recurrence linkage (nonconformity records referencing prior nonconformity IDs or same-category tags)
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: centralized nonconformity register with immutable timestamps, linked RCA documents, documented verification results, and logged policy/training updates.
Strong: automated linkage of corrective actions to audit findings and demonstrable closure evidence (e.g., updated policy/version history).
Weak: ad-hoc spreadsheets or email threads without consistent timestamps, missing RCA documents, or manual assertions of closure without verification evidence.
Weak: recurring nonconformities with corrective action records that lack verification or show repeated reopenings.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control mandates investigation, development and implementation of corrective actions, root cause analysis and verification — all characteristic corrective activities.

CPL-43.differentiation_note: This control specifically covers the end-to-end nonconformity lifecycle (detection → RCA → corrective action → verification → policy/training update) and therefore requires lifecycle timestamps, RCA completion evidence and recurrence tracking, unlike sibling controls that may focus only on detection or preventative configuration.

CPL-44.measurement_goal: Measure the completeness of the compliance register (count of identified legal/statutory/regulatory/contractual obligations), the percentage of those obligations mapped into policies/procedures and supplier contracts, the percentage of employees trained on those obligations, and the rate of audit findings and their remediation within defined policy targets.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is primarily a preventive control that requires coverage/coverage-percent style measures (e.g., percent of obligations mapped, percent of employees trained). Counts of obligations and trends over time (updates/audit findings) are useful secondary signals to show completeness and change.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: A queryable compliance register row count and source, percent of register entries that are mapped to a policy or contractual clause, percent of relevant employees who completed required compliance training, number and age of open compliance audit findings, and timestamps of last review/update per obligation to demonstrate ongoing monitoring and change handling.
data_signals: compliance register entries with source citation (law/regulation/contract) and unique ID
mapping table rows: obligation ID → policy/procedure document ID
supplier contract inventory rows with security/privacy clauses flagged
training assignment and completion records (employee ID, module, completion timestamp)
compliance audit findings dataset (finding ID, obligation ID, severity, open/closed status, remediation timestamps)
change log entries for regulatory/contractual updates (timestamp, author, rationale)
legal counsel or compliance review timestamps and sign-offs per obligation
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated, timestamped compliance register with source citations and formal mapping to policies and supplier contracts; training records with completion and assessment scores; audit findings logged with remediation timestamps.
Moderate: periodic audit reports and signed review memos but manual spreadsheets for the register.
Weak: ad-hoc/manual lists, missing mapping to policies, infrequent training records, and no change-log for regulatory updates.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control mandates identifying, documenting and integrating external obligations into policies and processes to prevent non‑compliance (phrases: 'identify, document, and comply', 'integrating these requirements into organizational policies').

CPL-44.differentiation_note: This control focuses on maintaining an authoritative register and mapping obligations into policies, training and supplier contracts (governance-level coverage), rather than technical detection or incident response—measurements must therefore emphasize register completeness, mapping coverage, training completion, and audit remediation metrics.

CPL-45.measurement_goal: Measure the occurrence and timeliness of independent ISMS reviews (scheduled vs completed), the count and severity of findings per review, and the percentage and time-to-resolution of recommendations against defined SLA targets so an auditor can verify reviews were impartial, conducted on schedule, and findings were remediated.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Counting reviews and findings (primary) demonstrates that independent reviews actually occur and produce observations; percentages capture remediation rate and SLA compliance; trending shows improvement over time as the control requires regular intervals and timely addressing of recommendations.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Track scheduled independent review events vs. completed review events (with auditor identity/independence), extract each review report's finding IDs and severity, and measure remediation action items (owner, created and closed timestamps). Calculate percent of findings closed within SLA and mean time-to-remediate by severity and report age.
data_signals: independent review schedule and completion timestamps
auditor identity/engagement letter or independence attestation
review reports with finding IDs, descriptions and severity/classification
remediation/action item tickets with owner, created_timestamp, closed_timestamp, status
percentage of identified issues resolved (resolved_count / total_findings) per review
time_taken_to_address_recommendations (remediation time per finding)
policy change log / new technology deployment records (timestamps) that trigger reviews
interview attendance logs or interview notes confirming personnel awareness
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: Signed external auditor reports + remediation tickets with timestamps showing closure within SLA and auditor independence attestations.
Strong: Consistent scheduled reviews recorded in a central calendar and automated tracking of finding lifecycle (created → assigned → closed).
Weak: One-off or verbal confirmations of reviews, undated PDF summaries without remediation ticket linking, or reviews performed by vested internal parties without independence evidence.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control mandates periodic impartial evaluation of the ISMS to identify vulnerabilities and ensure compliance, which is a detective auditing activity ("evaluated by an impartial party at regular intervals").

CPL-45.differentiation_note: This control focuses on periodic independent assessment and remediation-tracking (scheduling, auditor independence, findings closure) rather than day-to-day technical controls or continuous monitoring; measurement must link reviews to remediation tickets and SLA performance.

CPL-46.measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of information security policies (including topic-specific policies and standards) reviewed and approved on schedule, the percentage of employees who completed required policy/training acknowledgements, and counts/rates of reported policy violations and audit findings to demonstrate ongoing compliance with the ISMS.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This control requires demonstrating coverage and adherence (review completion, training completion) so percentage metrics best show coverage; counts and rates complement to show absolute violations and violation frequency and trends over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of policies reviewed within their defined review frequency (policy-level), percentage of policies with documented owner and approval timestamp, employee training/course completion rate and signed policy acknowledgements by role, plus counts of reported policy violations, audit findings tied to policy non‑compliance, and time-to-approve policy changes.
data_signals: policy register entries with policy id, policy owner, last review date, next review due date
policy change history records with change timestamp, approver id, and approval timestamp
policy review schedule and completed review timestamps
training enrollment and completion records (employee id, course id, completion timestamp)
policy acknowledgement receipts (employee id, policy id, signed/accepted timestamp)
policy violation/incident reports (incident id, policy referenced, report timestamp, severity)
audit and assessment findings referencing specific policies and remediation status
time-to-approve policy changes (change request timestamp → approval timestamp)
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated policy register and change-history logs with approver identities and timestamps, integrated training completion records and signed acknowledgements, and continuous logging of policy violations and audit findings.
Weak: ad-hoc or manual spreadsheets with occasional timestamps, annual attestations without proof of communication/approval, or one-off training rosters lacking per-employee completion evidence.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control requires establishing review processes, assigning responsibilities, communicating updates and training personnel to prevent non-compliance (phrases: 'establish a systematic review process', 'assigning responsibilities', 'training programs').

CPL-46.differentiation_note: This control focuses specifically on governance of policy lifecycle, approvals and personnel awareness (reviews, acknowledgements, training completion and tracked approvals) rather than technical access or vulnerability controls, so measurements must center on policy registers, approval workflows, and training records.

CPL-47.measurement_goal: Measure that every audit engagement has an approved audit plan and scoped systems, that auditors operate with enforced read-only access and secured devices, and that complete, tamper-evident audit trails and incident counts for audit periods are captured and reviewed.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This control emphasizes enforcement and coverage (e.g., read-only access, secured auditor devices, completed audit plans), so percentage metrics (coverage/compliance %) are primary; counts and rates (unauthorized access attempts, incidents) and trends (audit incidents over time) are useful secondary measures.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Track the percent of audits with an approved signed audit plan before testing, percent of auditor accounts configured with read-only roles for scoped systems, count and rate of unauthorized access attempts during audit windows, and audit-trail completeness (log records per audit session and presence of start/end timestamps). Include device attestation or inventory proof for auditor devices and training completion records for auditors and impacted operational staff.
data_signals: signed audit plan timestamp and approver ID
audit scope document listing critical systems and data in scope
auditor account role assignments (read-only flag) and role-change audit logs
authentication/authorization logs during audit windows with user ID, action, resource, timestamp
access control change logs showing attempted or completed changes during audit testing
audit trail/log completeness metrics (log start/end markers, sequence numbers, retention status)
auditor device inventory and device posture/attestation records (encryption, MDM status)
audit-related incident records and number of unauthorized access attempts during audit periods
training completion records for auditors and operational staff tied to the audit engagement
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated authentication and authorization logs correlated to an approved audit plan and audit session timestamps; read-only role enforcement visible in role-assignment records; device attestation (MDM compliance) for auditor endpoints.
Weak: one-off screenshots or emailed lists of auditors without machine-readable logs or signed plan metadata; manual notes without timestamped logs.
Strong: tamper-evident, sequential logs with retention status and integrity checks; Weak: ad-hoc log exports lacking context (no audit session IDs or scope links).
Strong: documented training completion tied to auditor ID and date before the audit start; Weak: generic training policy without proof of completion per auditor.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The description prescribes preventive measures (establishing an agreed audit plan, ensuring auditors have read-only access and secured devices) to prevent disruption or unauthorized changes; logging provides secondary detective capability.

CPL-47.differentiation_note: This control specifically targets protections and monitoring during audit testing (audit plans, read-only enforcement, device attestations, and audit-trail completeness) rather than general audit program controls or post-incident forensics—measurement must link artifacts to discrete audit engagements and audit windows.

CRY-91.measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of information transfers that use approved secure methods (e.g., TLS, AES-encrypted channels, VPN, encrypted file sharing), count data transfer incidents (breaches or unauthorized transfers), and track third‑party transfer agreement coverage and personnel training completion rates to demonstrate adherence to the Information Transfer policy.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This control is primarily preventive (policy, encryption, and required methods), so a percentage (coverage/compliance rate) best shows how much transfer activity meets policy; counts and rates (incident counts, transfer volumes) supplement to show absolute incidents and frequency; a score can aggregate multi-factor compliance (encryption + agreements + training).

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Compute the percent of all information transfer events that used an approved secure channel (TLS with approved ciphers, AES-encrypted file share, VPN) during the reporting period; count transfers that were unencrypted or used non‑approved methods, log data transfer incidents (breach/unauthorized access) with timestamps and remediation status, measure percent of third‑party agreements that include required security clauses, and percent of relevant personnel who completed transfer-security training within policy timeframe.
data_signals: information transfer/logging records with protocol and cipher details (e.g., TLS version, cipher suite)
system/application logs indicating transfer channel type (secure email, encrypted file share, VPN) and transfer timestamps
configuration records showing encryption enabled (AES/TLS settings) for transfer services
digital signature / certificate validation logs for signed transfers
data transfer incident reports and breach logs (unauthorized access, data loss events)
third‑party contract/agreement inventory with security clause presence and last review date
training completion records for personnel involved in information transfer (attendance, completion timestamps)
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated transfer logs with protocol/cipher details and peer certificate validation are strong evidence;
continuous DLP or proxy logs showing per-transfer encryption status are stronger than occasional manual checklists;
signed, versioned third‑party agreements and machine-verifiable certificate checks strengthen evidence;
relying only on policy documents or one-off training attendance without operational logs is weak evidence;

control_type_classification.reasoning: The description focuses on establishing rules, procedures, cryptographic protections (TLS, AES), access restrictions and agreements to prevent unauthorized disclosure, which are preventive measures.

CRY-91.differentiation_note: This control is specifically about secure transfer channels, cryptographic enforcement, third‑party agreement coverage and transfer-related training — unlike sibling controls that may focus on storage, access provisioning, or incident handling, it requires measurement of per-transfer encryption and contract coverage rather than only access lists or vulnerability counts.

CRY-92.measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of sensitive data and assets that are encrypted at rest and in transit, plus key management lifecycle health (generation, rotation, storage, destruction) and adherence to the organization's cryptographic policy and approved algorithm/protocol standards.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: The control is preventive and requires coverage verification (e.g., what portion of sensitive data is encrypted) so percentage is primary; counts (incidents, non‑compliant assets), policy/compliance scores, and trends (rotation frequency over time) are useful secondaries to show operational health and improvement.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Compute the percentage of classified-sensitive assets/data stores that have encryption-at-rest enabled and the percentage of services/endpoints that enforce encryption-in-transit (e.g., TLS). Measure key lifecycle events per key (creation timestamp, rotation timestamps, expiration/destruction timestamp), the count of keys stored in HSM/KMS vs. unprotected stores, and the number of cryptography policy non‑conformances or encryption-related incidents over the audit period.
data_signals: asset/data inventory with sensitivity/classification labels and encryption_at_rest flag
service endpoint TLS configuration scan results (protocols/ciphers enabled, TLS enforcement status)
vulnerability/configuration scan output listing deprecated/weak algorithms (e.g., RSA key sizes, non-AES usage)
key_management_event_logs (key generation, rotation, import, export, destruction timestamps)
key_storage_type mapping (HSM/KMS vs. file/keystore) per key identifier
cryptographic policy version, last_review_date, and audit findings (policy non-conformance records)
encryption_incident_count and incident reports referencing 'breach' or 'key compromise'
key_rotation_compliance ratio (keys rotated within required interval) and overdue_key_count
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: Continuous automated discovery and configuration scans showing encryption_at_rest flag true for >95% of classified-sensitive assets and TLS enforced for all external/internal endpoints; complete key lifecycle logs with HSM/KMS usage and recorded rotations per policy intervals.
Strong: Policy audit reports and system-enforced controls (e.g., KMS/HSM) demonstrating algorithm/key-length compliance.
Weak: Point-in-time screenshots, self-attestations without logs, annual manual tests only, or missing key rotation/management logs.
Weak: Use of deprecated algorithms or missing mapping between classified data and encryption status (i.e., inability to link sensitive labels to encryption flags).

control_type_classification.reasoning: The description focuses on ensuring data is encrypted and establishing policies/key management to prevent unauthorized access, i.e., preventive measures ('ensure that information is encrypted', 'key management strategy').

CRY-92.differentiation_note: This control specifically requires measuring encryption coverage and key lifecycle health (algorithm selection, rotation, storage), distinct from sibling controls that might focus on access or incident response; its unique need is linking data classification to cryptographic enforcement and key management telemetry.

DCH-106.measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of users who have received, acknowledged, and completed AUP-related onboarding and recurring training, and measure the count and rate of documented AUP violations (by type and asset) to demonstrate enforcement and behavior change.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: The control is preventive (policy, onboarding, awareness) so coverage/completion percentages are most useful to show compliance; counts and rates are needed to track violations and monitoring activity and trends to show improvement over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of active users who have a recorded AUP acknowledgment and completed required AUP training within the required timeframe; counts and timestamps of AUP violation incidents (by user, asset, violation type), mobile device enrollment/acceptance of AUP, and results of periodic compliance audits showing non‑compliance findings and remediation status.
data_signals: AUP acknowledgment records (user_id, timestamp, document version)
training/LMS completion records for AUP modules (user_id, course_id, completion_timestamp, score)
onboarding checklist records showing AUP distribution and acknowledgement (new hire id, timestamp)
recorded incidents of policy violations (incident_id, user_id, asset_id, violation_type, timestamp, disposition)
security monitoring alerts mapped to user behavior (alert_id, user_id, event_type, timestamp)
mobile device policy acceptance/enrollment logs (device_id, user_id, acceptance_timestamp)
periodic audit/compliance report entries (audit_id, finding, user_scope, remediation_timestamps)
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated AUP acknowledgment records and LMS completion logs with user IDs and timestamps are strong evidence vs. scanned PDFs or verbal attestations which are weak.
Continuous monitoring alerts correlated to user IDs and assets (with incident records) are stronger than single point-in-time manual audit findings.
Consistent onboarding records showing AUP distribution to every new hire (full_coverage) is stronger than ad-hoc communications.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The description mandates a documented AUP, communication, onboarding inclusion and training — actions that prevent misuse and unauthorized access.

DCH-106.differentiation_note: This control is human-centric (policy distribution, acknowledgments, training and behavior monitoring) rather than a technical access-control or vulnerability remediation control, so measurements must focus on user acknowledgments, training completion, and violation/event counts. If measurement systems are limited, note that some signals (e.g., behavior alerts) may be weaker proxies.

DCH-107.measurement_goal: Measure the coverage and correctness of data classification across inventories and repositories (percentage of assets/documents labeled by sensitivity tier), count and trend of classification-related incidents (misclassification, mishandling), and the frequency and completion of policy reviews and classification training against organizational policy requirements.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This control is preventive and best evidenced by coverage metrics (percentage of data labeled) to show policy implementation; counts and trends capture incidents and remediation velocity, while distribution shows classification spread across repositories.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Compute: (1) percentage of assets/documents in the canonical inventory and each major repository that have a classification label (public/internal/confidential/restricted) and timestamped label metadata; (2) count and timestamps of incidents where misclassification or mishandling was identified; (3) counts and dates of policy review approvals and staff classification training completions. Break these by asset type and business unit.
data_signals: asset/document inventory records with classification label and label timestamp
repository metadata showing label presence (e.g., file metadata, database column tag, object storage tags)
classification change history logs (who changed label, old/new label, timestamp, justification)
incident records tagged 'misclassification' or 'classification error' with root cause and remediation timestamps
policy review and approval records with dates and reviewer IDs
training completion records for classification protocol (user ID, completion date, curriculum ID)
DLP/classification tool enforcement logs (label-based enforcement actions, blocked transfers, encryption applied)
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated, timestamped classification metadata in the canonical asset inventory and enforcement logs from classification/DLP tools; regular (e.g., quarterly) policy review entries and automated training completion records.
Moderate: periodic audits showing sampled assets labeled correctly and incident records with remediation evidence.
Weak: one-off manual spreadsheets or ad-hoc labeling without change history, paper-based training sign-offs, or annual-only classification exercises.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control mandates policies, labeling, handling, training and integration to prevent inappropriate handling of sensitive data, indicating a preventive control.

DCH-107.differentiation_note: This control focuses specifically on the taxonomy, labeling, and governance of data (coverage and correctness) rather than downstream access controls or incident response, so measurements must emphasize label metadata, classification change history, and training/policy review cadence.

DCH-108.measurement_goal: Measure the coverage and accuracy of applied classification labels across the authoritative information asset inventory (by asset type and classification level) and track the count and rate of labelling errors or unlabeled assets and related incidents over time to demonstrate consistent application of the labelling scheme.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is primarily a preventive control; percentage metrics (e.g., % assets labelled) demonstrate coverage against the classification scheme, while counts (mislabelled/unlabelled items) and trends show volume and change over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of assets in the canonical information inventory that have a valid classification label (public/internal/confidential/restricted), count of assets/files found unlabelled or mislabelled during audits, timestamps of label application relative to asset creation, and number of incidents or breach events tied to mislabelling. Also measure training completion rates for staff responsible for labelling where applicable.
data_signals: authoritative information asset inventory records with unique asset ID and classification_label field
file/record metadata containing sensitivity/classification tags and label_timestamp
labelling tool/application logs showing user_id, tool_id, label_change events, and timestamps
audit sampling results: expected_classification vs observed_label and error_reason
incident/issue tickets referencing mislabelled or unlabelled data and associated breach indicators
training completion records for labelling procedures and attestation logs
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated metadata labels applied at ingest with immutable label_timestamp and labelling-tool audit logs covering >90% of assets.
Moderate: regular sample-based audit results showing high accuracy and linked remediation tickets.
Weak: one-time spreadsheet inventories, manual screenshots, or training records without verification of label application.
Strong linkage between mislabel incidents and incident tickets (shows chain of evidence); absence of such linkage weakens evidence.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control prescribes classification and labelling procedures to ensure appropriate handling and to prevent misuse or unauthorized access, which is preventive in nature.

DCH-108.differentiation_note: This control specifically requires measuring label coverage and labelling accuracy across the information inventory (labels, timestamps, tool logs and audit error counts), unlike sibling controls that might focus on access enforcement, incident handling, or vulnerability remediation.

DCH-109.measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of organizational records that meet defined protection requirements (encryption, access controls, and backup) and the counts/rates of evidence showing integrity and handling compliance — specifically: % of sensitive records encrypted, backup success rate and restore-test success rate, % of records with correct retention/disposal events, and count of unauthorized access or falsification incidents per period.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: The control is primarily preventive and coverage-oriented (encryption, access controls, retention) so percentage metrics (coverage/compliance rates) best show whether records meet policy; counts/rates capture incident volumes (unauthorized access, backup failures) and trends show improvement or regression over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Track a records inventory joined to protection attributes: record classification label, encryption flag, associated ACL/entitlement, latest backup execution and checksum, and retention/disposal event timestamps. Measure percent of records that are encrypted where required, backup success and restore-test success rates, count of unauthorized access attempts and confirmed falsification incidents, and percent of records with completed disposal per retention schedule.
data_signals: records inventory with classification labels and unique record IDs
encryption status/algorithm flag per record or storage container
access control lists (ACLs) or entitlement assignments tied to record IDs
authentication and access logs (user ID, timestamp, action, source IP) for record accesses
backup job logs and results (timestamp, job ID, success/failure, checksum/hash)
restore test results and timestamps (successful restores, test frequency)
retention schedule entries and disposal event logs (timestamped deletion/transfer actions)
audit trail entries showing modifications, creation, deletion, and the responsible principal
evidence_strength_indicators: Continuous machine-generated logs linking record IDs to access events and changes (strong) vs. ad-hoc screenshots or manual spreadsheets (weak)
Automated backup logs plus periodic successful restore test records (strong) vs. only scheduled backup configuration without execution proof (weak)
Cryptographic verification/checksums showing integrity versus absence of integrity hashes (strong)
System-enforced encryption flags and configuration records (strong) vs. undocumented claims of encryption (weak)

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control emphasizes safeguards (encryption, access controls, backups, retention/disposal procedures) to prevent loss, unauthorized access, or falsification, although it also references audit trails for detection.

DCH-109.differentiation_note: This control specifically targets records-level protection and retention/disposal tracking (encryption, backups, retention events and audit trails) rather than broader system hardening or network defenses; its unique measurement need is mapping protection attributes to a records inventory and retention/disposal evidence.

DCH-110.measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of records/devices scheduled for deletion that are securely and permanently deleted within the defined retention timelines, plus counts of deletion failures and recoverable-data incidents; and measure coverage of automated vs manual deletion methods and documented proof of destruction.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: The control requires verifying deletion compliance against retention timelines and policy (coverage/percent complete), while counts and rates capture failure volumes and remediation velocity and trend shows ongoing compliance over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of assets/records assigned a deletion deadline and the subset actually deleted by that deadline (grouped by data classification and storage type), deletion method used, timestamps of deletion jobs, verification artifacts (overwrite hashes or certificates of destruction), and counts of incidents where deleted data was subsequently recovered or recovery was attempted.
data_signals: deletion job execution timestamps per asset/object
asset/object unique identifier and data classification
retention schedule / deletion deadline field per asset
recorded deletion method (physical destruction, data wiping, crypto-erase)
verification/proof-of-destruction artifacts (overwrite hashes, disposal receipts, certificates)
deletion log entries with actor (automated job ID or operator) and status codes
count and details of incidents/data recovery attempts referencing deleted assets
coverage metric: number of assets under automated deletion tooling vs total assets subject to deletion
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: continuous automated deletion logs with verification artifacts (overwrite hashes, certificates of destruction) and audit trails mapped to retention schedules
Strong: per-asset retention/deletion deadline fields tied to automated job outcomes proving deletion occurred by deadline
Weak: one-off manual deletion reports or annual attestations without per-job logs or proof-of-destruction
Weak: screenshots or ad-hoc notes without verifiable timestamps, identifiers, or verification artifacts

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control mandates establishing policy, timelines and deletion methods to prevent unauthorized retention and exposure (phrases: 'ensure ... removed when it is no longer needed', 'policy', 'timelines for deletion').

DCH-110.differentiation_note: This control specifically requires measurable proof of secure destruction (methods, verification artifacts and schedule compliance); unlike sibling controls that may address access or classification, it requires per-asset deletion evidence and sanitization proof.

DCH-111.measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of identified sensitive data elements that are masked across databases, applications, and during transmission, plus counts of accesses to unmasked data and incidents of exposure; track masking technique usage and masking deployment across environments (production vs non-production).

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is a preventive control focused on coverage and consistency; percentage metrics (e.g., % of sensitive elements masked) directly show coverage while counts and rates capture access events and exception frequency and trends show improvement over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of sensitive data elements (by table/column/field) and count of those elements with an applied masking technique; number of unmasked access events (user, role, timestamp, environment); masking job execution records (tool, timestamp, success/failure); list of approved exceptions and environments where masking is not applied (e.g., non-production). Compare masking coverage by asset and environment against policy-required coverage.
data_signals: sensitive data element inventory with classification tags (table/column/field)
masking implementation records mapping data element → masking technique (tokenization/encryption/substitution)
masking deployment environment flag (production/non-production/test)
automated masking tool job execution logs with timestamps and success/failure status
access logs showing views of unmasked data (user id, role, timestamp, justification)
exception/approval records for unmasked data access
incident reports referencing exposures of unmasked sensitive data
periodic masking review/audit reports and policy alignment evidence
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: continuous automated masking job logs plus a complete data inventory mapping data elements to applied masking techniques and environment; access logs showing zero unauthorized unmasked views.
Weak: one-time manual screenshots, ad-hoc masking performed in a single environment, or absence of a data inventory and exception approvals.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The description explicitly calls data masking a 'preventive control designed to protect sensitive information' and focuses on preventing unauthorized interpretation of data.

DCH-111.differentiation_note: This control focuses specifically on measuring masking coverage, techniques applied, and unmasked data access events across systems and environments, unlike sibling controls that might focus on classification, retention, or encryption-only controls.

DCH-112.measurement_goal: Measure the coverage of sensitive-data classification and DLP enforcement (data in motion, at rest, and in use) as a percentage of total critical assets, track counts and rates of DLP-detected and DLP-blocked incidents by channel, and measure false positive rate and mean time to remediate DLP incidents to demonstrate prevention and monitoring effectiveness.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: DLP is primarily a preventive control that requires coverage metrics (e.g., % of sensitive data/assets classified and protected). Counts/rates of blocked detections and trends in false positives/MTTR are useful secondary evidence of detection and corrective activity.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percent of assets and data stores with sensitivity labels and active DLP protection (by data state: in motion, at rest, in use); counts of DLP detection events and blocked transmissions by channel (email, web upload, cloud storage, removable media) with user, device, timestamp, and policy id; false positive/false negative annotations and MTTR for DLP incidents linked to incident tickets. Include DLP rule versions and deployment/agent coverage status to show enforcement.
data_signals: data classification inventory with sensitivity labels and asset identifiers
DLP policy definitions and policy version/change history
DLP event logs: detection and blocking events (timestamp, user id, source ip/device id, destination/channel, rule id, matched content pattern)
counts of blocked outbound transfers by channel (email, web, cloud, removable media)
