false positive and false negative annotations (labelled by analyst) and false positive rate
endpoint/agent deployment and coverage status report (per host/workload)
incident/ticket records linked to DLP alerts with detection-to-remediation timestamps (for MTTR)
integration/configuration records showing connections to firewalls, endpoint protection, CASB or cloud gateways
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: Continuous, timestamped DLP event logs showing blocked actions with contextual attributes (user, device, destination), full agent coverage reports, and low false positive rate with linked incident tickets and remediation timestamps.
Weak: Periodic manual reports, ad-hoc incident narratives, or policy documents without enforcement logs or agent coverage data.
Weak: High false positive rate or missing linkage between DLP events and incident remediation tickets reduces confidence.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The description emphasizes preventing unauthorized transmission/disclosure and enforcing policies (preventive), while also mentioning identification/monitoring (detective) — prevention is primary.

DCH-112.differentiation_note: Unlike access-control or incident-response controls, this control requires measurement of data-classification coverage across data states and enforcement efficacy (blocked transfers, false positive rate, agent coverage) rather than solely access provisioning or response timelines.

END-40.measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of user endpoint devices that meet mandatory endpoint security requirements (encryption enabled, EDR installed and healthy, configured to security baselines, and enrolled in management), plus counts of unmanaged or non-compliant devices and MFA enrollment rates to demonstrate preventive coverage.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is a preventive control focused on coverage/compliance (e.g., 'mandatory encryption', 'all devices are accounted for'), so percentage metrics (coverage, compliance %) best show whether policy requirements are met; counts and rates (unmanaged devices, update frequency) supplement for operational detail.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Compute device-level compliance ratios and counts: percentage of managed endpoints with encryption enabled, percentage with EDR agent installed and reporting healthy, percentage compliant with the defined security baseline, count of unmanaged or unaccounted devices, and percentage of users enrolled in MFA/training. Include timestamps to show currency (last-seen, last-patch, agent heartbeat) and map devices to asset criticality.
data_signals: endpoint inventory records (device id, owner, device type, last-seen timestamp)
encryption status flag per device (data at rest and data in transit enforcement logs)
EDR agent installation and health telemetry (agent version, last-checkin, heartbeat, quarantine events)
configuration compliance scan results against security baselines (policy deviation flags, baseline score)
OS/patch level and last update timestamp per device (security update deployment records)
MDM/endpoint management enrollment status and enrollment timestamps
authentication/MFA enrollment logs and unauthorized access attempt logs (failed logins by device)
user training and awareness completion records tied to device owners
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: continuous MDM/EDR telemetry and automated inventory showing real-time enrollment, agent health, encryption flags, and last-patch timestamps.
Strong: automated baseline compliance scan results and remediation tickets linked to device IDs.
Weak: occasional manual inventory spreadsheets or one-off screenshots of device settings.
Weak: annual training records without mapping to current device ownership or device presence.

control_type_classification.reasoning: Control text prescribes preventive measures (mandatory encryption, access controls, regular updates, asset discovery) to stop compromise before it occurs.

END-40.differentiation_note: This control is focused on device-level preventive coverage (inventory, encryption, EDR, baseline compliance and user training) rather than detection or incident response; measurement must emphasize coverage percentages and managed-enrollment counts rather than alert volumes.

END-41.measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of endpoints with up-to-date, active malware protection (EPP/antivirus) and application whitelisting enabled, plus counts and timing of malware detections and remediation actions (containment, eradication, recovery) to demonstrate coverage and preventive effectiveness.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: As a preventive control the key auditor question is coverage (what percent of endpoints are protected and up-to-date), so percentage is primary; counts of detections and trending of remediation/definition-update activity are useful secondary metrics to show operational effectiveness and change over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: A dashboardable metric showing: (a) total endpoints vs endpoints with an active, up-to-date EPP/antivirus agent and application whitelisting enabled; (b) count of malware detection events by endpoint and signature with timestamps; and (c) remediation lifecycle timestamps (detection → containment → eradication → recovery) per incident to calculate time-to-contain and time-to-recover.
data_signals: endpoint inventory (asset ID, OS, ownership, criticality)
EPP/antivirus agent installed flag and agent version per endpoint
antivirus definition/version and last-update timestamp per endpoint
EPP agent heartbeat / last-checkin timestamp
malware detection events (event id, signature/name, IOC, endpoint id, timestamp, severity)
incident/ticket records with containment, eradication and recovery timestamps
application whitelisting enablement status per endpoint
user awareness/training completion records referencing malware/phishing topics
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: continuous automated EPP telemetry (agent heartbeats, definition update logs) and SIEM/IDS detection events correlated to incident tickets with containment/eradication/recovery timestamps.
Strong: automated coverage reports showing near-100% endpoint agent presence and current definitions.
Weak: annual/manual penetration test or one-off spreadsheet attestations without agent telemetry or detection logs.
Weak: screenshots or point-in-time attestations that cannot be correlated to continuous telemetry or remediation timestamps.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The description repeatedly instructs installing/updating antivirus/EPP on all endpoints and preventing unauthorized execution (application whitelisting), which are preventive measures.

END-41.differentiation_note: This control is focused on endpoint preventive coverage and up-to-date defenses (coverage percent and agent/update telemetry), rather than purely detection or incident response metrics; measurement must emphasize continuous asset coverage and definition currency in addition to detection counts.

GOV-89.measurement_goal: Measure the completeness and timeliness of the organization's context analysis: percent of identified internal and external issues reviewed on the scheduled cycle, count of context-derived actions created and closed within policy SLAs, and coverage of stakeholder engagements mapped to ISMS objectives alignment scores.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is a preventive control requiring coverage and timeliness measures (percent of context items reviewed/covered). Counts (number of issues/actions) and trends (review frequency over time) and an alignment score are useful secondary indicators to show activity and effectiveness over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Track entries in the context register (issue id, internal/external classification, description), last review timestamp, assigned owner, action(s) created with SLA and remediation/closure timestamps, stakeholder engagement records linked to specific context items, and an ISMS objectives alignment audit score. Produce percent-complete metrics (e.g., percent of context items reviewed within the required review cycle) and counts of open vs closed actions by age and priority.
data_signals: context register entries with created and last_review timestamps and internal/external classification
number of identified internal and external issues (by quarter)
records of stakeholder engagements (meeting minutes, communications) linked to context items
action items generated from context analysis with assigned owner, SLA target, and closure timestamp
ISMS objectives alignment / audit findings referencing context updates (alignment score or findings count)
frequency of context reviews (review schedule vs actual review dates)
policy or regulatory requirements mapping records showing updates after context reviews
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: a continuously maintained context register with timestamps for creation and last review, documented stakeholder engagements linked to specific context items, and actions with SLA and closure timestamps showing sustained closure rates.
Weak: ad-hoc memos or one-off narrative reports without timestamps, or periodic audit statements without linkage to a maintained context register and action tracking.

affinity_keywords: Understanding the organization and its context

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control requires proactively identifying and reviewing internal/external factors and integrating them into the ISMS and risk management process (phrases: 'identify and analyze', 'context register', 'regularly reviewing').

GOV-89.differentiation_note: This control is focused on measuring the quality and cadence of context analysis outputs (context register, stakeholder engagement, alignment scores) rather than operational controls like logging, access, or technical vulnerability remediation.

GOV-90.measurement_goal: Measure completeness of the interested‑parties register (count), the percentage of stakeholder requirements that have been mapped into ISMS policies/procedures, and stakeholder satisfaction/engagement frequency to demonstrate that stakeholder needs are identified, prioritized, integrated and reviewed on schedule.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is a preventive governance control focused on coverage and integration; percentage metrics (e.g., % of requirements integrated) show coverage/completeness, while counts (number of parties/requirements) and trends show change over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of interested parties in the register with attributes (type, contact, last-reviewed timestamp); count of documented stakeholder requirements; mapping records linking each requirement to an ISMS policy/procedure or risk treatment; percentage of requirements addressed/implemented; timestamps of reviews/engagements and stakeholder satisfaction survey scores.
data_signals: interested parties register entries (party type, contact, date added, last_review_date)
stakeholder requirement records (requirement_id, text, source, priority, date_collected)
mapping table of requirement_id -> ISMS policy/procedure/control_id
policy/procedure revision records referencing stakeholder requirements (document_id, revision_date)
stakeholder engagement logs (surveys, interviews, workshops) with timestamps and attendance
stakeholder satisfaction survey responses and calculated satisfaction score
action-item or remediation ticket list tied to stakeholder requirements with status and closure timestamps
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: an automated, timestamped stakeholder register and explicit mapping records linking each stakeholder requirement to ISMS policy or control plus recent stakeholder survey results and closed action items.
Moderate: documented requirement lists and meeting minutes without systematic mapping to policies or without timestamps for periodic review.
Weak: ad‑hoc notes, one‑time workshop slides, or an outdated register with no evidence of integration or follow‑up.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The description requires identification, documentation and integration of stakeholder needs into the ISMS (establishing processes), which is characteristic of a preventive governance control.

GOV-90.differentiation_note: This control is focused specifically on identifying and tracking stakeholder requirements and their integration into the ISMS (register completeness and mapping), rather than on policy content, incident handling, or project‑level security tasks — measurement must prove mapping and review cadence rather than only policy existence.

GOV-91.measurement_goal: Measure ISMS coverage and operationalization: percentage of required ISMS artifacts (policies, scope statement, risk assessments, risk treatment plans) that are current, approved, published and communicated; count of completed risk assessments and percentage of risk treatment actions implemented on schedule; percentage of employees completing mandated ISMS training and number of management reviews/internal audits completed within scheduled periods.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This control is governance/preventive and requires coverage and compliance measures (e.g., percent of policies published, percent of staff trained), so percentage metrics best express coverage and adherence; counts and audit scores are useful secondary signals to show activity and control effectiveness over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: 1) Percentage of required ISMS documents (policy, scope statement, risk assessments, risk treatment plans) that are approved, published, and distributed within the last policy-review period; 2) Count and status of risk assessment records and % of risk treatment plan actions implemented within defined target timelines; 3) % of employees who completed ISMS training within the reporting period and count of management reviews/internal audits held with tracked corrective actions.
data_signals: ISMS scope document/version and approval timestamp
risk assessment records with asset mapping, date, and assessor identity
risk treatment plan entries with action owner, due date, and implementation status
policy documents metadata (version, approval timestamp, publication/distribution list)
training completion records from LMS (user, course id, completion timestamp)
management review minutes/attendance and decisions logged with dates
internal audit reports with findings, severity, and corrective action status
security incident register counts and incident classifications (timestamped)
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: centralized, timestamped records (policy repository, LMS logs, risk register) with approval and distribution metadata and tracked corrective actions.
Strong: internal audit reports showing findings mapped to risk treatment plan items with closure evidence and dates.
Weak: one-off attestations or undated/manual spreadsheets of policies or training completions.
Weak: anecdotal management statements without meeting minutes or tracked action items.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control focuses on establishing, implementing, maintaining and improving an ISMS (policies, roles, scope, objectives), which is primarily preventive governance to avoid security lapses.

GOV-91.differentiation_note: This control is an overarching ISMS governance control (policy, scope, risk assessment, training and management review metrics) rather than a narrow technical control; its measurement needs are composite governance indicators (coverage, approval, training completion, audit outcomes) rather than single-system telemetry.

GOV-92.measurement_goal: Measure the degree to which top management demonstrates leadership for the ISMS by (a) the percentage of organizational units with approved ISMS objectives aligned to corporate strategic objectives, (b) the count and timeliness of top-management ISMS review meetings and decisions, and (c) the percentage of projects and processes that have information security requirements integrated at initiation.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is a preventive leadership-level control focused on coverage and alignment (best expressed as percentages of units/projects in compliance). Counts of reviews and trend/score metrics are useful secondary measures to show activity and improvement over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percent of business units with documented and approved ISMS objectives that map to stated strategic goals; number and dates of top-management ISMS review meetings (with attendance and decisions recorded) in the last 12 months; percent of new projects whose project initiation documents include identified information security requirements; budget/resource allocation records for ISMS activities vs. planned requirements.
data_signals: approved information security policy document (with approval timestamp and approver identity)
ISMS objectives register mapped to strategic direction (objective → strategic goal mapping)
management review meeting records/minutes (date, attendees, decisions, action items)
project initiation documents or project registers with security requirement flag
budget/expense records showing ISMS resource allocations and approvals
employee engagement/survey responses on information security awareness and leadership communication (participation rate, scores)
role/responsibility assignments for information security (RACI or role assignment list)
records of continuous improvement actions opened/closed from ISMS reviews (action item timestamps)
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: signed policy and objectives with timestamps, contemporaneous management review minutes listing attendees and actions, project intake forms containing security requirement fields, and consistent budget approvals for ISMS resources.
Weak: informal or anecdotal statements of leadership support, one-off presentations without documented decisions, or annual statements without supporting meeting minutes or mapped objectives.

affinity_keywords: align with the organization's strategic direction

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control requires top-management to establish policy, objectives, allocate resources and integrate security — actions that prevent governance and process failures, so it is primarily preventive.

GOV-92.differentiation_note: This control is leadership/strategy-focused and requires evidence of policy approval, management review activity, objective-to-strategy mapping and resource allocation — unlike operational controls it measures governance coverage and management engagement rather than technical system settings.

GOV-93.measurement_goal: Measure policy governance and dissemination: percent of employees who have acknowledged the current information security policy, frequency and timeliness of policy reviews and approvals by top management, and counts of policy-related training completions and policy-derived security incidents to show the policy is documented, communicated, accessible, and driving compliance.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is primarily a preventive governance control where coverage and adherence matter (e.g., % of employees who have acknowledged the policy, % of policy review cycles completed). Counts (number of approvals, incidents) and trends (review cadence over time) are useful secondary views to show activity and improvement.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Track the policy version history and approval timestamps; compute the percentage of active personnel with a recorded acknowledgement/attestation for the current policy version; count completed training events that reference the policy and count policy review events within the mandated review interval; link security incidents or audit findings that cite policy violations to show effectiveness.
data_signals: policy_document_version_id, effective_date, and approver_id (policy version history)
policy_approval timestamps and approving top_management identifiers
policy_publication records (publication_date, distribution_list, publication_location/intranet URL)
employee acknowledgement/attestation records (user_id, timestamp, policy_version_id)
policy-related training completion records (course_id referencing policy, user_id, completion_date)
policy_review log or meeting minutes (review_date, reviewer, outcome/actions)
audit findings and compliance review records referencing the policy (finding_id, severity, remediation_status)
security incident records linked to policy clauses (incident_id, timestamp, clause_violated)
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated acknowledgement records and timestamps tied to a specific policy version plus signed/recorded top management approval and repeatable review logs; training completion records with course IDs that reference the policy; audit findings mapped to policy clauses.
Weak: single static PDF on a shared drive without versioning or approval metadata, manual emails without acknowledgement tracking, or anecdotal attestations that employees have 'read' the policy.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control establishes a documented, communicated policy and objectives (prevention of breaches/non-compliance) and requires management action (drafting, approval, communication), which are preventive activities.

GOV-93.differentiation_note: This control focuses on governance-level documentation, approval, communication, and employee acknowledgement metrics (policy coverage and review cadence), unlike operational controls that measure technical detection or remediation — its unique measurement need is tracking versioned publication and organisation-wide acknowledgement/coverage.

GOV-94.measurement_goal: Measure the proportion of roles relevant to information security that have documented responsibilities and authorities assigned and the presence/frequency of formal reporting on ISMS performance to top management (e.g., % of roles with signed assignment + count of management reports submitted on schedule).

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This control requires coverage/assignment and communicated evidence — auditors want coverage percent (how many applicable roles have assignments) as the primary indicator; counts of missing assignments and trends in reporting completeness are useful secondary signals.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Compute the percentage of all roles identified as 'relevant to information security' that have a documented responsibility and authority statement, a timestamped assignment, and evidence of communication/acknowledgement. Also count ISMS performance reports delivered to top management per reporting cycle and number of roles without assignments or without acknowledgement.
data_signals: role inventory (role_id) with 'information security responsibility' field and classification of relevance
job description documents or role profiles with version and dated responsibility/authority clauses
signed role-acceptance or acknowledgement receipts (electronic signatures, signed forms) with timestamps
email/policy distribution logs or publication records showing recipients and timestamps for communicated assignments
management review minutes and ISMS performance reports (dated) submitted to top management
change log of role assignments (who assigned, when, and what changed)
training/acknowledgement records linked to assigned roles (evidence of communication awareness)
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: machine-readable role inventory plus dated signed acknowledgements and periodic management review reports proving recurring reporting.
Moderate: documented job descriptions with dated revisions but no signed acknowledgement or distribution logs.
Weak: unaudited verbal confirmations or undated policy documents without recipient records.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control mandates assignment and communication of responsibilities (a preventive governance action); it also requires reporting which provides verification but the primary purpose is prevention through clear role allocation.

GOV-94.differentiation_note: This control is governance-focused on assigning and communicating role-level responsibilities and reporting to top management, unlike operational controls that measure task execution or technical configuration — it requires measuring documented role coverage and evidence of communication rather than technical logs.

GOV-95.measurement_goal: Measure the inventory and coverage of documented information security objectives across relevant functions/levels, the percentage that are measurable and linked to policy/risk assessment, and counts of objectives with assigned owner, resources, completion dates and monitoring/communication records.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This control requires counting discrete artifacts (objectives, assignments, action items, communications) to demonstrate coverage and monitoring; percentages and trends are useful secondaries to show coverage rates and updates over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: An objectives register listing each information security objective with attributes: owning function/level, linkage to information security policy, linkage to risk assessment/risk treatment, measurable flag/metric, assigned owner, required resources, planned completion date, last monitored/review timestamp, and evidence of communication/published documentation. Also measure counts of objectives created, updated, monitored, communicated, and closed within reporting periods.
data_signals: documented objectives register or policy document identifiers
objective attributes: measurable (boolean) and measurable metric definition
linkage/reference to information security policy id
reference to risk assessment or risk treatment record ids
owner assignment fields (responsible person/role) for each objective
planned completion date and actual completion timestamp for objectives
monitoring/review logs or review meeting minutes with timestamps
communication records or publication timestamps (intranet post, email distribution lists)
document retention or version-control audit trail for objectives
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: a maintained objectives register with structured fields (owner, measurable metric, policy/risk links), automated monitoring/review timestamps and published communication records.
Strong: objective-level linkage to specific risk assessment entries and evidence of resources allocated/budget lines.
Weak: a single high-level policy statement without per-function objectives, or only annual manual statements without timestamps or owner assignments.
Weak: screenshots or one-off reports without versioned documented information or clear linkage to risk assessments.

affinity_keywords: consistent with the information security policy
how the results will be evaluated

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control explicitly requires objectives to 'be monitored' and 'be measurable', indicating detection/monitoring activities rather than purely preventive actions.

GOV-95.differentiation_note: Unlike operational controls, this governance control requires measuring documented objective artifacts and their planning attributes (owner, resources, completion dates, communication and monitoring) across functions, not technical configuration or incident counts; focus is on completeness and traceability of objectives. (Selected incident_response and human_risk_and_training focus areas reflect related risks about breach response contacts and communication.)

GOV-96.measurement_goal: Measure the existence and completeness of a documented ISMS communication plan (mapping what/when/with whom/how) and the percentage of planned communications executed on schedule and acknowledged by the intended internal/external audiences.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is a preventive control requiring coverage and adherence (existence and completeness of communications), so percentage metrics (coverage / on-time delivery rates) are most useful; counts (number of communications, missed communications) and trends (timeliness over time) provide supporting context.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Track the ratio of planned ISMS communications that were executed and acknowledged on their scheduled dates, the coverage of stakeholder groups defined in the communication plan, and time from policy/change approval to publication and audience acknowledgement. Also verify the presence of documented entries mapping message (what), schedule (when), audience (with whom), and channel (how).
data_signals: documented communication plan entries (message description, intended audience, channel, scheduled date) -- communication_plan.csv or doc
policy or ISMS document revision and approval timestamps with approver identifier
publication timestamps (intranet post publish time, email send timestamps) correlated to planned schedule
delivery acknowledgements / read receipts / explicit acknowledgements or training completion records tied to the specific communication
stakeholder register / distribution lists showing roles and contact targets
project plan artifacts referencing information security communications or requirements
log of missed or overdue scheduled communications (instances and reasons)
communication channel logs (email server send logs, intranet audit logs) showing actual transmission events
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated publication timestamps + delivery/read acknowledgements tied to documented communication plan entries and approver-signed policy revision records.
Strong: continuous tracking of scheduled vs executed communications with stakeholder coverage metrics.
Weak: presence of a communication plan document alone without execution timestamps or acknowledgements.
Weak: ad-hoc or one-off emails without linkage to a documented 'what/when/with whom/how' entry.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control requires the organization to 'determine the need' and define what/when/with whom/how to communicate, which is a preventive planning activity.

GOV-96.differentiation_note: This control focuses specifically on planning and executing ISMS communications (mapping what/when/with whom/how) rather than policy creation, training content, or incident notifications; measurements must link documented communication plan entries to execution and audience acknowledgement.

GOV-97.measurement_goal: Measure the completeness and lifecycle status of ISMS documented information: percentage of required ISMS documents that exist, are approved/published, have assigned owners, and have up‑to‑date review dates; and count of documents missing approval, owner, or overdue review.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is a preventive control focused on coverage and completeness of required documentation so percentage metrics (coverage / compliance rate) best show compliance; counts (inventory, missing items) and trends (review timeliness over time) are useful secondary measures for remediation and improvement.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: A documentable registry query that returns: total number of ISMS-required documents (per the standard/policy), number and IDs of those with approved status, publication date, assigned owner/role, last review timestamp and next review due date, and any distribution/publication evidence (URL or distribution list). Also measure number of project plans referencing required security policies and number of documents with missing owners or overdue reviews.
data_signals: ISMS documented information inventory (document IDs mapped to required-list C.7.5.1)
document metadata: status (draft/approved/published/archived)
approval records: approver ID and approval timestamp
publication evidence: document URL/portal publication timestamp or distribution list entries
document owner/role assignment fields
last review timestamp and next review due date
overdue review flag / days since last review
project plan references to Information Security Policies
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: centralized document register with structured metadata (status, approver, timestamps, owner, review schedule) and automated publication logs.
Moderate: periodically exported spreadsheets of documents with reviewers and dates but lacking automated publication proof.
Weak: ad-hoc PDFs or email attachments without approval timestamps, missing owner fields, or stale review dates.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control mandates documented information be present and maintained for ISMS effectiveness ("shall include...documented information"), which prevents gaps by requiring artifacts and lifecycle management.

GOV-97.differentiation_note: This control uniquely focuses on existence, approval, publication and review lifecycle metadata of ISMS documents (coverage and timeliness), rather than operational implementation or technical control performance; measurement must emphasize document inventory and review timeliness.

GOV-98.measurement_goal: Measure the completeness and approval status of documented information by counting records that include required identification metadata (title, date, author or reference number), format/media fields, and an associated review-and-approval record; report the count and percentage of documents missing any required metadata or lacking documented approval within policy timeframes.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Detective measurements should enumerate non‑compliant items (count of documents missing metadata or approval). Percentages provide coverage context and trending shows improvements in documentation hygiene over time, matching the control's requirements to verify identification, format/media and review/approval.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of documented information records and the subset that include required identification fields (title, date, author or reference number), format/media attribute (language, software version, graphics, paper/electronic), and a recorded review-and-approval entry (approver id and timestamp). Also measure time-between-creation-or-update and approval to detect missing or delayed approvals.
data_signals: document metadata records (title, date, author, reference number)
document format/media field (language, software version, graphics, paper/electronic)
document version history and create/update timestamps
approval records (approver id, approval timestamp, approval status or signature)
document inventory listing with classification or owner
audit trail of create/update events (user id, timestamp, change summary)
time delta between document creation/update and approval
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated document management system logs showing metadata populated, version history, and approver id+timestamp for each document change.
Strong: system-enforced required metadata fields and approval workflow entries for each published document.
Weak: ad-hoc file shares or point-in-time screenshots of documents without audit logs or approval metadata.
Weak: manual attestations or annual attestations alone that do not show ongoing create/update audit trails.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control requires verification that documents have identification/format/media and review/approval records, which implies detection/validation of documentation metadata and approval trails.

GOV-98.differentiation_note: This control focuses specifically on metadata completeness and recorded approval events for documented information (title/date/author/format/media/review), unlike sibling controls that might focus on content controls, distribution, or retention; measurement must emphasize metadata fields and approval audit trails rather than document content. Focus area choices include incident_response and change_management as adjacent areas because incomplete documentation can delay breach response and arise during changes.

GOV-99.measurement_goal: Measure the coverage and compliance of ISMS-required documented information against organizational document control policy: percentage of required documents that are under formal control (stored in the authorized repository, have access controls, version history, retention/disposition records and preservation status) and counts of documents missing required controls (e.g., no version control, no retention date, or not identified as external origin).

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is a preventive control focused on coverage and compliance so percentage metrics (document coverage/compliance rates) are most useful; counts identify exception volume and trends show whether control adoption and retention compliance are improving or regressing.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Compute the percent of ISMS-required documents present in the authorized document repository with complete metadata (owner, classification, version identifier, last modified timestamp, retention/disposition entry, preservation status) and the count of documents lacking one or more required controls (missing version history, no access control entries, not flagged as external-origin when applicable). Also measure number and percent of documents with expired or overdue retention/disposition actions and count of ad-hoc/unmanaged document locations.
data_signals: document inventory records with metadata fields (owner, classification, version, last_modified)
repository storage location / file path and preservation status (legibility/preservation flag)
access control lists / permissions for each document
version history/change control logs (timestamps, author, version id)
retention and disposition records (retention expiry date, disposition action, disposition timestamp)
external-origin document identifier/flag
document distribution/share audit logs or distribution lists
counts of documents in unmanaged locations (email attachments, personal drives) vs. authorized repository
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: centralized document inventory with authoritative metadata and automated linking to version history, access-control policies, and retention/disposition records (machine-readable fields).
Strong: automated audit logs of distribution and access showing enforcement of ACLs and change events.
Weak: one-off manual spreadsheets listing documents without links to repository, or single point-in-time screenshots of a folder structure.
Weak: undated documents or documents lacking version history, retention entries, or approval/sign-off evidence.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control text requires documents 'shall be controlled' and prescribes activities (distribution, access, version control, retention) which are preventive measures to avoid confidentiality, integrity, and availability failures.

GOV-99.differentiation_note: This control uniquely targets formal control and lifecycle management of ISMS documented information (inventory, versioning, retention/disposition and preservation), rather than controls about contacts, roles, or project-level security; measurement must focus on document metadata, repository coverage and retention compliance rather than incident or personnel metrics.

GOV-100.measurement_goal: Measure the existence and execution of a documented monitoring & measurement program: count of defined information security processes/controls with assigned measurement methods, the number of measurement executions and analysis events in the reporting period, and the count/percentage of measurement results documented and acted on by assigned personnel.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is a detective governance control: auditors need counts of defined measurements, executions, analyses and assigned responsibilities (count). Percentages (coverage/completion) and trends (improvement over time) are useful secondary views; scores can summarize overall ISMS measurement maturity.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of monitored information security processes and controls that have documented measurement methods; timestamps of measurement executions and analysis events; identity of assigned monitor/analyst for each measurement; stored measurement results and evaluation reports; count of follow-up actions (remediation/improvement) opened as a result of evaluations.
data_signals: documented monitoring and measurement plan or register (list of monitored controls/processes)
formal measurement methods and procedures (documented method descriptions)
measurement execution timestamps and frequency records
records of who performed the measurement/analysis (assigned person or role)
analysis and evaluation reports or meeting minutes documenting results
documented evidence of results (reports/files) retained as 'documented information'
remediation or improvement ticket IDs created from measurement findings
coverage count: number of controls/processes with defined measurement vs total controls
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated measurement logs with timestamps and assigned user IDs, a maintained monitoring register mapping controls to measurement methods, and periodic analysis reports showing follow-up actions.
Weak: one-off manual snapshots, unsigned/undated documents, or anecdotal statements without retained measurement records.

affinity_keywords: when the monitoring and measuring shall be performed
effectiveness of the information security management system

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control requires determining what is monitored/measured and evaluating performance and effectiveness, which are detective activities that produce evidence of control operation.

GOV-100.differentiation_note: This control governs the measurement program (what/how/when/who) and evidence retention for ISMS performance, rather than mandating a specific technical control or remediation SLA — its unique need is measurement coverage, documented methods, and assignment of responsibilities.

GOV-101.measurement_goal: Measure the proportion and timeliness of planned top-management information security management system (ISMS) reviews completed, the presence of documented minutes/decisions and attendance by top management, and the closure rate and age of action items resulting from those reviews.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: The control is detective/oversight oriented and auditors need rates (e.g., on-time review completion rate) plus percentages (coverage/attendance) and counts (number of reviews, action items) to demonstrate recurring reviews and outcomes over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of scheduled management review events in the audit period vs. count completed; percent of reviews completed within the planned interval; presence of meeting minutes linked to each review; attendance record showing top management participation; number of action items opened during reviews and the percentage closed within defined timeframes. Include timestamps for scheduling, execution, and action-item resolution to enable timeliness queries.
data_signals: management review schedule/calendar entries with planned interval and timestamps
meeting minutes/management review reports linked to schedule entries
attendance lists showing Top management presence (names/roles and timestamps)
management review decision records or approved outputs (policy update references)
action item register from management reviews (action id, owner, created date, due date, closed date, status)
counts of reviews planned vs completed in period
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: recurring automated calendar entries + signed minutes and attendance records for each planned interval, and action-item lifecycle data showing assignment and timely closure.
Weak: single-point artifacts such as a one-off presentation or an annual high-level statement without minutes, attendance, or tracked action items.
Strong: linkage between review outcomes and documented policy updates or risk treatment actions; Weak: undocumented verbal confirmations or retrospective summaries without timestamps.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control requires periodic reviews by top management to ensure suitability/adequacy/effectiveness — an oversight/detection activity rather than a preventative or corrective action.

GOV-101.differentiation_note: This control focuses on management-level review cadence, documented outcomes and action-item tracking (attendance, minutes, closure rates) rather than operational controls like policy creation or project-level security activities; measurement must emphasize scheduled review completion and governance outputs.

GOV-102.measurement_goal: Measure the completeness and timeliness of management review inputs: percentage and count of action items from prior reviews closed on schedule, counts and trends of nonconformities and corrective actions considered, number of audit findings and their closure rate, and the percent fulfillment of defined information security objectives reviewed during each management review cycle.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: The control is detective and centers on enumerating review inputs (action items, nonconformities, audit findings) so raw counts are primary; percentages (closure rates, objectives met) and trends over time are useful secondary measures to show effectiveness and improvement.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Number of management review meetings and associated artifacts; count of action items opened in prior management reviews and their closure timestamps and owners; count of current nonconformities and corrective actions with creation and remediation dates; audit findings count and remediation status; metrics showing fulfilment status of each information security objective; count of interested-party feedback items logged and addressed; risk assessment outputs and status of risk treatment plan items with due dates and completion flags.
data_signals: management review minutes / meeting records with date and agenda
action item tracker rows (id, origin review date, owner, due date, status, closure timestamp)
nonconformity and corrective action register entries (id, severity, open date, close date)
audit reports and findings list with remediation status and timestamps
information security objective records with target metrics and current fulfilment percentage
risk register entries and risk treatment plan items with status and completion timestamps
interested-party feedback logs (source, date, summary) and response/action records
monitoring and measurement result reports referenced in the review (metric name, period, value)
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: structured management review minutes linked to an action-item tracker and risk register, with timestamps, owners, and closure evidence for prior actions; audit and monitoring reports cited and attached.

Weak: single narrative or unsigned minutes, no linkage to action tracker or risk register, verbal confirmations without timestamps, or isolated annual summaries that don't show per-cycle closures or trends.

affinity_keywords: changes in external and internal issues

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control requires management to review and consider past actions, monitoring results, audits and risk assessments—activities that detect and evaluate past/ongoing performance rather than directly prevent events.

GOV-102.differentiation_note: This control focuses specifically on inputs to management review (action-item status, audit/monitoring trends, risk treatment status and stakeholder feedback) rather than on executing the individual processes (e.g., performing audits or risk assessments), so measurements must link review artifacts to underlying evidence (action trackers, audit reports, risk register).

GOV-103.measurement_goal: Measure the number and schedule-compliance of management review meetings and the presence and closure rate of documented decisions and action items (continual improvement opportunities and ISMS change needs) recorded as evidence of management reviews.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This detective review control is best evidenced by counts (number of management reviews, decisions, and action items). Percentages (on-schedule completion, action-closure rate) and trends (improvement in closures over time) provide complementary context about completeness and progress.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of management review events in the reporting period, count of documented decisions recorded per review, count of action items created with assigned owner and due date, and action-item closure rate and time-to-close. Also measure presence of documented ISMS changes (revision entries) and distribution/communication records for review results.
data_signals: management review meeting minutes / documented results (timestamped)
list of decisions recorded from each management review (text entries)
action item records with owner, creation timestamp, due date, and closure timestamp
ISMS revision history entries linked to management review decisions
schedule of planned management reviews and actual review timestamps
evidence of communication/publishing of review results (distribution list, email/publication timestamp)
status of identified continual improvement opportunities (open/closed)
repository access logs for documented information (who uploaded/approved minutes)
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: Automated, timestamped meeting minutes and action-item tracker with assigned owners and closure timestamps; linkage between a review decision and the ISMS revision history.
Strong: Audit trail showing publication/distribution of results and approvals.
Weak: Single undated summary document or verbal attestations without action-item assignment or closure evidence.
Weak: Infrequent/manual copies of minutes with no traceable connection to ISMS changes or communication records.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control requires documenting the results of management reviews and making evidence available, which is a periodic/detective activity that demonstrates review and decision-making rather than preventing incidents directly.

GOV-103.differentiation_note: Unlike controls that mandate policy creation or project-level security tasks, this control specifically requires measurable documentation of management review outcomes and tracked action items (decisions and ISMS change needs) — measurement must focus on minutes, decision records, and action-item closure rather than policy content alone.

GOV-104.measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of planned ISMS continual improvement actions completed on schedule, the trend in ISMS effectiveness indicators (policy review compliance, audit nonconformities closed), and the rate of new improvement items opened vs. closed per review cycle to demonstrate ongoing improvement of suitability, adequacy and effectiveness.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is a preventive control focused on coverage and completion of improvement activities (best evidenced by percentages of completed actions and policy-review compliance). Trending and counts are secondary to show improvement over time and volumes of actions/ findings; maturity/ effectiveness can be represented as a score.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Compute: (1) percentage of ISMS improvement/CAPA actions completed by their due date per management review cycle, (2) number and closure rate of audit nonconformities and corrective actions, (3) percentage of required policy reviews performed and published with approver and date, and (4) ISMS effectiveness/maturity score trend across reporting periods.
data_signals: improvement/CAPA item records with owner, created_date, due_date, status, closed_date
internal audit findings and nonconformity records with open/close timestamps and severity
policy versioning log entries (policy id, version, review_date, approver, publish_date)
management review meeting minutes and meeting_date (agenda items referencing ISMS actions)
counts of improvement actions opened vs closed per review cycle
ISMS performance or maturity assessment scores by period
project plan artifacts indicating inclusion of information security activities or risk treatments
responsibility registry showing defined/allocated information security roles
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: structured CAPA/improvement trackers with timestamps, owners, and closure evidence plus repeated management review minutes and versioned policy records; demonstrates continuous improvement trend.
Weak: one-off statements or unsigned/manual notes, ad-hoc email confirmations, or isolated reports without timestamps/owners; these do not show ongoing, measurable improvement.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control mandates continual improvement of the ISMS (prevention via ongoing governance and process improvements) as stated 'shall continually improve the suitability, adequacy and effectiveness of the information security management system.'

GOV-104.differentiation_note: This control focuses on process- and governance-level continual improvement (policy reviews, CAPA action tracking, ISMS effectiveness metrics) rather than operational security controls like access configuration or vulnerability scanning.

GOV-105.measurement_goal: Measure the existence and approval status of information security and topic-specific policies, the percentage of relevant personnel and interested parties who have received and acknowledged each published policy, and the rate at which policies are reviewed within planned intervals or after significant changes.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: The control requires measuring coverage (e.g., acknowledged/published/reviewed) across target populations and policies, so percentage best captures completeness; counts and trends are useful secondary signals for volume and change over time and a score can aggregate policy health.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: A policy registry query that returns per-policy: policy_id, topic, approval timestamp and approver id, publication URI, assigned policy owner, planned review date, actual last review timestamp/version, and acknowledgement records (user_id, timestamp, identity) for all relevant personnel and interested parties; compute % of target population with acknowledgement and % of policies reviewed on schedule.
data_signals: policy registry entries (policy_id, title, topic-specific flag)
management approval records (approver_id, approval_timestamp, approval_evidence)
publication metadata (publication_timestamp, publication_location/URI)
acknowledgement records (policy_id, user_id, acknowledgement_timestamp, acknowledgement_method)
policy owner assignment (policy_id -> owner_id / responsibility assignment)
policy review history/version log (scheduled_review_date, actual_review_timestamp, change_reason)
list of relevant interested parties / distribution list for each policy
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated, timestamped acknowledgement logs mapping user_id to policy_id and version + versioned policy registry with approval metadata and scheduled vs actual review dates.
Strong: presence of approver identity and signed approval records stored in the registry.
Weak: single-point manual attestations (e.g., one-off emails) or undated/unsigned policy documents without version history.
Weak: anecdotes or undocumented claims that policies were communicated without distribution or acknowledgement records.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control text includes 'type: detective' and focuses on verification activities (acknowledgement, review evidence and publication) rather than purely preventative configuration.

GOV-105.differentiation_note: This control uniquely requires measuring policy lifecycle and human acknowledgement coverage (approval, publication, distribution, review adherence) rather than technical configuration or incident metrics; focus area mapping emphasizes governance and training because the main measurement is policy communication and acknowledgement.

GOV-106.measurement_goal: Measure the coverage and effectiveness of management's enforcement of information security policy by reporting: (1) percentage of personnel who have acknowledged the current information security policy and topic-specific procedures, (2) percentage of roles/job descriptions with defined and allocated security responsibilities (including segregation of duties), and (3) counts of policy publications/communications and project plans that include security requirements.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is a preventive management control requiring 'all personnel' to apply policies; auditors need coverage percentages (acknowledgement, role coverage). Counts and rates (e.g., publications, communications per period) and trends over time are useful secondary evidence.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Calculate: (A) percent of active employees with a recorded policy acknowledgement for the current information security policy or topic-specific procedure (acknowledgement timestamp tied to policy version); (B) percent of active roles/job descriptions that explicitly list assigned information security responsibilities or segregation-of-duties mappings; and (C) counts of policy publications/communications and number of project plans containing security checkpoints within a defined period.
data_signals: policy repository records with publish date, version, and owner
policy acknowledgement records (employee id, policy id/version, acknowledgement timestamp)
employee directory / HR active headcount to compute percentages
job description records indicating defined security responsibilities (role id, responsibility flags, version)
segregation-of-duties / role-mapping documents showing allocated duties
communication/training logs (email distribution records, intranet announcement timestamps, assigned policy training completions)
project plan templates or project intake records showing security included (project id, security checklist flag)
policy exception or waiver records (number and justification)
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated acknowledgement records tied to specific policy version and timestamps, versioned policy repository entries, role/job-description records in HR system, and project intake records with security checklist completion.
Weak: single one-off all‑hands emails without acknowledgement tracking, verbal directives, undated or unversioned policy documents, or inconsistent job descriptions across systems.

control_type_classification.reasoning: Text states 'Management shall require all personnel' and mandates application of policies and procedures, which is a preventive management directive to ensure compliance.

GOV-106.differentiation_note: This control is focused on management enforcement and personnel-level adoption (acknowledgements, job-role responsibilities, communications) rather than technical configuration or detection — measurement must capture policy distribution, acknowledgement, and assignment of responsibilities.

GOV-107.measurement_goal: Show that the organization maintains a current, reviewed roster of relevant authorities with documented points-of-contact and formal or operational communication channels; measure the percentage of required authorities with a documented contact record reviewed within the policy review period, plus counts of formal agreements or test communications executed.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: As a preventive control the key measure is coverage/compliance (percentage of relevant authorities with an up-to-date contact record); counts of agreements and trending of review recency are useful secondary indicators of maintenance over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of relevant authorities that have a documented contact record (POC name, role, contact method) with a last_review_timestamp within the policy-defined review interval; count of signed/recorded agreements or formal communications with authorities; timestamps of the most recent test/notification to each authority.
data_signals: authorities_contact_list entries (POC name, organization, role, contact_method)
contact_list last_review_timestamp / review completion records
signed MOUs or formal agreements metadata (document id, signed_date)
communication logs to authorities (notification emails, incident notification entries, call logs) with timestamps
scheduled review records and review completion attestations
policy publication / distribution logs referencing authority contacts
regulatory notifications, audit findings, or fine records related to failure to contact authorities
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: a maintained contact registry (structured data) with last_review_timestamp within policy interval, linked agreement documents, and recorded successful test/notification events to those authorities.
Weak: a single static document or one-off email listing authorities without structured timestamps, no review history, or only verbal/undocumented contacts.
Strong: presence of signed agreements/MOUs and routine test notifications; Weak: only policy text stating contacts should exist but no operational logs or attestations.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The wording 'shall establish and maintain' is proactive and aims to prevent failures to engage authorities, indicating a preventive control.

GOV-107.differentiation_note: This control specifically requires documented external authority contacts and their ongoing maintenance (POC records, agreements, review timestamps), unlike sibling controls that might focus on internal policy definition or personnel training; measurement must therefore focus on contact registry coverage and review cadence rather than internal policy content alone.

GOV-108.measurement_goal: Measure the existence, currency, and frequency of formal contacts and information exchanges with relevant special interest groups and the degree to which those engagements produce actionable security guidance incorporated into policies, project plans, or defined responsibilities.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Auditors need coverage/uptake measures (percentage of required groups engaged or policy updates influenced) as primary proof; counts of engagements and trending of contact frequency/remediation over time provide complementary evidence of ongoing maintenance and improvement.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count and percent coverage of required special-interest groups with active agreements or documented contacts; timestamped records of last engagement per group; number of threat bulletins/inputs received from those groups and the count of policy or project-plan changes that cite or incorporate those inputs. Track meeting/minutes frequency per quarter and percentage of required engagements completed within policy-defined periods.
data_signals: registry of special interest group memberships / MOUs with last_signed_date
contact directory entries with last_contacted_timestamp and role/affiliation
meeting invitations and minutes (date, attendance list, agenda items)
received threat bulletins / intelligence feeds with ingestion timestamps
policy revision logs with rationale or source references linking to group input
project plan artifacts indicating security input sourced from interest group engagement
communication logs/distribution records for guidance shared with personnel (dates, recipients)
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: up-to-date signed MOUs/membership records plus quarterly meeting minutes and ingested threat bulletins showing incorporation into policy or project artifacts.
Strong: measurable linkage from an external bulletin to a documented policy or project-plan change with timestamps.
Weak: a single outdated contact list, sporadic informal emails with no meeting minutes, or anecdotal claims of contact without documented artifacts.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control aims to ensure ongoing contacts and information sharing to prevent lack of threat awareness and policy gaps, so it functions primarily to prevent risks.

GOV-108.differentiation_note: Unlike policy-definition or project-plan controls, this control requires measurement of external engagement artifacts (MOUs, meeting minutes, received bulletins) and linkage evidence showing those engagements influenced policies or project security inputs; the entry compensates for the empty description by using associated risk texts as the keyword/evidence source.

GOV-109.measurement_goal: Measure the proportion of required operating procedures for information processing facilities that are (a) formally documented with metadata and approval, and (b) accessible to the personnel who need them; additionally track currency by measuring time-since-last-review and count of procedures missing approval or access.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is a preventive control that mandates documentation and availability: coverage and accessibility are best represented as percentages (e.g., % of procedures documented and accessible). Counts (missing/overdue procedures) and trending (review cadence over time) are useful secondary views.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: A dataset that lists every operating procedure required for each information processing facility with fields: document identifier, scope (facility/system), version, approval status, approver and approval timestamp, publication/distribution record, access permissions, last review date, and current status. From that: compute percent documented, percent accessible to required personnel, count of procedures missing approval, and average days since last review.
data_signals: procedure document metadata (document id, title, version, scope/facility mapping)
approval records (approver id, approval timestamp, approval signature or audit entry)
publication/distribution records or repository publish timestamp
access control lists or group permissions mapping procedures to personnel/roles
document access logs showing user id and read/access timestamps
last review date and reviewer for each procedure
procedure lifecycle status (draft/approved/retired)
count of procedures mapped per information processing facility
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: centralized document repository with structured metadata, recorded approval audit trails, automated publication timestamps, ACLs mapping procedures to roles, and access logs showing personnel reads.
Moderate: repository entries with version and review dates but manual attestations of distribution.
Weak: point-in-time screenshots, ad-hoc local files, unsigned documents, or verbal attestations without approval or access records.

control_type_classification.reasoning: Control text states procedures 'shall be documented and made available' which is a preventive requirement to ensure personnel have required documentation.

GOV-109.differentiation_note: This control specifically focuses on documentation, approval metadata, and availability/accessibility of operating procedures for processing facilities (coverage and access metrics), rather than measuring training effectiveness, incident response performance, or the substantive content quality of procedures.

HRS-52.measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of personnel carrying out information-security‑relevant work who have documented, role-mapped competence (education, training, or experience), plus counts of competence-acquisition actions and the effectiveness (assessment scores or evaluation outcomes) of those actions.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is a preventive control requiring coverage and conformance (percentage of people with required competence). Counts (training completions), scores (assessment/evaluation results), and trends (improvement over time) are useful supplements to show acquisition and effectiveness.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: The ideal metric is the percentage of in-scope personnel (those performing work that affects information security) with a matching documented competence record (role competency definition + evidence such as training completion, certificate, education or experience) and passing evaluation results; supplemented by counts of competence-acquisition actions (training enrollments/completions) and average assessment scores by role.
data_signals: role-to-competence mapping / job competency definitions (documented per role)
training completion records with timestamps and course identifiers
certificates, education or experience records linked to employee IDs
post-training evaluation scores or assessment results
records of actions taken to acquire competence (enrollments, external training invoices, apprenticeship records)
retained documented information evidence files (HR file references, document storage paths, signed attestations)
background verification results where used to establish competence or trust
list of in-scope personnel (employees/contractors) and their assignment to information-security-affecting roles
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated HR/LMS records that map employee ID → role → competency definition → timestamped training completion → assessment score; these provide continuous, auditable traces.
Moderate: centrally stored scanned certificates and HR attestations with dates but without assessment scores.
Weak: one-time manager attestations or undocumented verbal claims; absence of documented evidence or only ad-hoc training attendance lists.

affinity_keywords: take actions to acquire the necessary competence
evaluate the effectiveness of the actions taken

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control text uses preventive language: 'determine the necessary competence', 'ensure that these persons are competent', and 'take actions to acquire', indicating prevention of competence gaps before they cause security issues.

HRS-52.differentiation_note: This control focuses specifically on measuring documented role-based competence and the effectiveness of competence-acquisition actions (training/assessment evidence), unlike sibling HR controls that may focus on background checks, termination obligations, or policy publication; if no closer focus_area exists, measurement centers on documented competence mapping and evaluation outcomes.

HRS-53.measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of persons performing work under the organization's control who have (a) received and acknowledged the information security policy, (b) completed role-appropriate awareness training, and (c) passed an assessment or attested to understanding their contribution to the ISMS and the implications of non-conformance; report counts of overdue or missing acknowledgements and average assessment scores.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This preventive control requires coverage measurement (who is aware) so percentage (coverage / completion rate) is primary; counts of missing acknowledgements and assessment scores provide additional context about scale and effectiveness.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Calculate: (1) % of staff/contractors who have an acknowledgement timestamp for the information security policy; (2) % who completed required awareness training within the required window; (3) distribution & mean of post-training assessment/quiz scores; (4) count of persons with overdue or missing attestations and any termination-related confidentiality acknowledgement records.
data_signals: policy acknowledgement receipts with user id and timestamp
training enrollment and completion records (LMS) with course id, user id, completion timestamp
post-training assessment/quiz scores by user id and timestamp
list of personnel in scope (HR roster) with role and employment status
signed confidentiality or termination acknowledgement records with timestamp
overdue/exception reports showing users missing required training or acknowledgements
training refresh cadence / last training timestamp per user
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated LMS completion logs + explicit policy acknowledgement receipts tied to HR roster and role mapping, with timestamps and course/acknowledgement IDs.
Strong: periodic assessment score trends showing improvement after training and documented refresh cadence.
Weak: one-off email notices or manual statements without timestamps or per-user acknowledgement, or aggregated claims with no user-level records.
Weak: HR lists without linkage to training/acknowledgement records or missing termination confidentiality attestations.

affinity_keywords: Persons doing work under the organization's control
benefits of improved information security performance

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control mandates awareness and understanding to prevent non-conformance (phrases: 'Persons... shall be aware', 'implications of not conforming'), so it is preventive.

HRS-53.differentiation_note: This control focuses specifically on awareness, training completion, policy acknowledgement and assessment evidence (coverage and effectiveness), unlike sibling HR controls that address background checks, termination procedures, or policy creation; measurement must therefore emphasize LMS/acknowledgement linkage to the HR roster rather than hiring or policy-document workflows.

HRS-54.measurement_goal: Measure the proportion of organizational roles with documented, approved and communicated information security responsibilities, plus the completion rates of role-proportional background checks and termination confidentiality communications to demonstrate responsibilities are defined, allocated and enforced.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is primarily a preventive control requiring coverage/assignment verification (percentage of roles covered). Counts (number of roles missing documentation), trends (coverage over time) and rates (background-check completion rates) provide useful secondary context tied to HR and policy lifecycle requirements.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: The ideal metric is percent of active roles that have: (a) a documented information-security responsibility entry in the role/job description or RACI matrix, (b) an approved/published policy reference and date, (c) a recorded acknowledgement by role-holder, and (d) required background-check completion or termination confidentiality step recorded. Each should be measurable per role ID and timestamped to allow verification of coverage and recency.
data_signals: role responsibility matrix / RACI records with role IDs and timestamps
job description documents tagged with 'information security' responsibilities and approval metadata
signed role-holder acknowledgements or policy acknowledgement audit logs
HR background verification records (completion status, level/scope, date) mapped to role
termination checklists showing confidentiality commitments communicated and signed (date)
policy publication and approval records (policy name, version, publish date, approver)
project plan artifacts indicating assigned security responsibilities (project ID, assigned role)
asset custody or allocation records tied to role (evidence of authorized asset handling)
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: centralized authoritative RACI and HR systems recording role responsibilities, acknowledgements, background-check status and termination checklist entries with timestamps and unique identifiers.
Strong: automated cross-reference between HR user records and role matrix showing 100% mapping.
Weak: ad-hoc emails, informal lists, or undated documents that cannot be mapped to current employees/roles.
Weak: annual manual attestations alone without timestamps or evidence of review/approval.

affinity_keywords: Staff acquire, use, or dispose of assets in an unauthorized manner

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control mandates defining, allocating and communicating responsibilities and HR checks (preventive actions) to reduce insider and policy risks.

HRS-54.differentiation_note: This control is specifically about defining, approving and communicating role-level information security responsibilities and HR lifecycle checks (hiring/background and termination confidentiality), unlike sibling controls that might focus on training delivery, operational procedures, or technical access enforcement.

HRS-55.measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of user accounts, roles, and business processes that comply with defined segregation-of-duties (SoD) rules; count active SoD conflicts and approved exceptions; and track time-to-remediate or resolve segregation conflicts to demonstrate enforcement of segregation requirements.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: As a preventive control, auditors need coverage metrics (percentage of assignments compliant with SoD) as primary evidence; counts of conflicts, remediation rates and trending show volume and improvement over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Calculate: (1) percent of active user-role assignments that violate defined SoD rules; (2) count of active SoD conflicts by severity/impact; (3) number of approved SoD exceptions with expiration and their proportion of total conflicts; and (4) mean time to remediate or remove conflicting assignments after detection. Measurements should be grouped by system/application and role criticality.
data_signals: role definition inventory with explicit SoD constraints (role-to-privilege mappings)
current user-to-role / user-to-privilege assignment records with timestamps
SoD rule engine violation logs and blocked-assignment events
SoD exception approval records (approver, rationale, expiry date)
periodic SoD review reports and review timestamps
privileged account list and assignment change audit logs
remediation/assignment change timestamps (time from detection to remediation)
termination and access removal logs (evidence of deprovisioning)
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated RBAC/ABAC enforcement with rule-engine violation logs and assignment-block events; continuous inventory of roles and automated SoD checks.
Medium: documented periodic SoD review reports and documented exception approvals with expirations and remediation plans.
Weak: one-off spreadsheets or ad-hoc reviews without audit trails, or only manual attestations without timestamped enforcement logs.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control uses mandatory language 'shall be segregated' and requires structuring responsibilities to prevent conflicts, indicating a preventive control.

HRS-55.differentiation_note: This control uniquely requires measuring active role/assignment enforcement and detected conflicts (SoD rule violations and exceptions), unlike sibling controls that may focus on background checks, policy publication, or termination procedures.

HRS-56.measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of candidate hires who have completed background verification prior to joining, the coverage and appropriateness of verification level relative to role information-classification, and the rate of scheduled ongoing re-checks and exceptions (waivers) over time.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: As a preventive control, auditors need coverage metrics (percent complete / percent compliant) showing the share of hires screened and mappings of screening level to role sensitivity; counts and rates (e.g., overdue checks, exceptions) provide operational detail and trends show ongoing adherence.
