NET-94.differentiation_note: This control specifically targets management of outbound/external website access (coverage and blocking of malicious content) rather than broader network controls or endpoint hardening, so measurements must focus on web-filter coverage, blocked-malicious events, and documented exceptions.

OPS-7.measurement_goal: Measure the proportion of operational processes that have defined acceptance/control criteria and documented execution records, the percent of planned changes processed through the change control workflow (approved, implemented, reviewed), and counts/rates of unintended changes that were reviewed and mitigated to closure.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: The control requires coverage and compliance with defined process criteria and documentation (best measured as percentages of processes/changes in compliance). Counts of unintended changes and trends over time are useful secondary metrics to show incidents and improvement.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percent of operational processes that have: (a) formal process criteria defined, (b) documented execution records with owner and timestamp, and (c) documented handling for externally provided processes; percent of planned changes logged in the change system with approval, implementation, and post-change review records; count and closure rate of unintended changes and associated mitigation actions and timestamps.
data_signals: process definitions / documented criteria records (documented information)
process execution logs / runbooks with owner and timestamp
change request records (CR ID, submitter, approver, approval timestamp)
change implementation timestamps and implementation owner
post-change review reports and remediation/mitigation actions with closure status
inventory of externally provided processes/products/services and associated contracts/SLA/SOC evidence
incident reports for unintended changes including root-cause and mitigation status
assignment of responsibilities / role-to-process mapping documents
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: centralized change-management system logs (CR lifecycle with timestamps, approvers, implementation and post-change review) and an up-to-date process inventory tied to owners and documented criteria.
Strong: supplier contracts and recent third-party assessment reports linked to the externally provided process inventory.
Weak: ad-hoc email approvals, hand-written meeting minutes, or annual-only documentation without timestamps or traceable approvals.
Weak: point-in-time attestations without historical change review records that show mitigation and closure.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control requires planning, establishing criteria, controlling processes and planned changes (phrases: 'plan, implement and control the processes' and 'control planned changes'), which are preventive controls to avoid adverse effects.

OPS-7.differentiation_note: This control is process- and change-centric (establishing criteria, documented execution, and change control including third-party processes), distinct from technical detection controls — measurement must focus on documentation, change lifecycle coverage and mitigation closure rather than event/alert counts.

PES-72.measurement_goal: Measure the coverage of required physical perimeter controls across sites (percentage of sites/assets with mandated perimeter controls implemented), the count and rate of unauthorized perimeter access or breach attempts, and the time-to-detect and time-to-respond for perimeter alarms and incidents.
risk_categories: Access control, Physical and environmental security

metric_type_preferences.rationale: As a primarily preventive control, auditors need coverage / compliance proportions (percentage) of perimeter controls; counts and rates of breaches/attempts and trends provide detective/corrective evidence about residual risk and response performance.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: 1) Percentage of sites/assets that have implemented the organization's required perimeter controls (physical barriers, badge readers, CCTV, intrusion alarms, environmental sensors) per policy. 2) Count and rate of perimeter-related unauthorized access or breach events and alarm activations. 3) Detection and response timestamps for perimeter alarms (time-to-detect, time-to-respond) and inspection/maintenance records for perimeter devices.
data_signals: site asset registry with perimeter control attributes (presence/absence of fence, gates, badge readers, CCTV, alarms)
physical access control logs (badge swipes, door open/close events) with timestamps and user IDs
intrusion alarm event logs with timestamps and resolution/response records
CCTV coverage inventory and footage retention logs / camera uptime metrics
perimeter inspection and maintenance records (inspection timestamps, findings, remedial actions)
unauthorized access / breach incident reports (incident timestamp, location, summary)
environmental sensor alerts relevant to perimeter (flood/fire/temperature) with timestamps
mean time to detect (MTTD) and mean time to respond (MTTR) for perimeter alarms
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: continuous automated access-control and alarm logs correlated with CCTV and response timestamps showing real-time detection and documented responses.
Strong: complete site registry demonstrating full policy-required perimeter control coverage (no missing devices) and recent inspection records.
Weak: annual manual inspection reports alone or sporadic snapshots of CCTV images without corresponding logs/timestamps.
Weak: anecdotal incident descriptions without correlated system logs or timestamped response records.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control title 'Physical security perimeters' indicates implementation of barriers/controls to prevent unauthorized access, which is primarily preventive in nature.

PES-72.differentiation_note: This control focuses specifically on physical perimeter devices and monitoring (coverage, alarms, inspections, CCTV) rather than logical access controls or equipment redundancy; because the control description is empty, keywords and measurement signals are derived from the control name and associated physical/environmental risk descriptions.

PES-73.measurement_goal: Measure coverage and effectiveness of entry controls for all designated secure areas: percentage of secure areas with approved entry controls in place, count of unsecured/untested access points, and number of physical access incidents (breaches) per period.
risk_categories: Access control, Physical and environmental security

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is a preventive control focused on coverage: auditors want percent coverage of secure areas with entry controls (primary). Counts of exceptions and trending of incidents/remediation over time provide additional context and demonstrate effectiveness.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: A queryable secure-area inventory (unique area IDs and classification) joined to an access-point inventory showing implemented entry controls (e.g., badge readers, locks, manned guard, CCTV). From that, compute: (1) percentage of secure areas where every defined access point has an approved entry control; (2) count of access points without controls; and (3) count and details of physical access incidents affecting secure areas (timestamp, area, method of entry).
data_signals: secure area inventory (area_id, designation/classification, owner)
access-point inventory mapped to secure areas (point_id, door/location, expected control type)
entry-control configuration records (device_id, control_type: badge_reader/lock/turnstile/guard, installation_date, status)
access-control system logs / badge-swipe logs (timestamp, user_id/credential_id, door_id, result: granted/denied)
visitor sign-in/escort logs (timestamp, visitor_name, escorted_by, area_accessed)
CCTV coverage mapping and footage retention records for secure areas (camera_id -> area_id, last_maintenance)
physical security incident reports and breach logs (incident_id, area_id, incident_type, timestamp, remediation actions)
maintenance and test records for entry-control devices (device_id, last_test_date, test_result)
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: continuous automated badge-swipe logs and CCTV footage correlated to an authoritative secure-area inventory and access-point inventory showing device configurations and recent successful tests.
Strong: up-to-date device maintenance/testing records and zero open access-point exceptions against the inventory.
Weak: one-off manual walkthrough checklists or annual attestations without device logs or mapped inventories.
Weak: visitor sign-in books alone without electronic access records or mapped entry-control configurations.

control_type_classification.reasoning: 'Shall be protected by appropriate entry controls' indicates a preventive requirement to implement controls that block unauthorized physical entry.

PES-73.differentiation_note: This control focuses specifically on physical entry control coverage for designated secure areas (inventory, access points and devices) rather than on incident response or electronic authentication metrics; measurement must map area-to-access-point-to-device and emphasize full coverage and device health.

PES-74.measurement_goal: Measure the proportion of offices, rooms and facilities that have documented physical security designs and implemented controls (e.g., access controls, environmental protections, and equipment protection), plus counts of facilities missing required controls or with outstanding deficiencies.
risk_categories: Access control, Physical and environmental security

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is a preventive control requiring coverage across all facilities — percentage coverage of facilities with implemented physical security is the most direct measure; counts of non-compliant sites and a compliance score (audit rating) are useful supplements.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percent of facilities (offices/rooms) with a current documented physical security design and evidence of implementation per policy (e.g., active badge access, CCTV coverage, environmental controls, UPS/fire suppression). Also measure count of facilities with missing or overdue physical-security implementations and the number of unresolved physical-security findings from recent inspections.
data_signals: facility inventory with identifiers and classification (office/room/facility)
documented physical security design artifacts per facility (design documents, diagrams, control lists) with timestamps
access control system records: badge readers installed list and last-seen device health/status
door/open sensor/tamper event logs by facility
CCTV camera coverage matrix and camera operational status logs
environmental sensor logs (temperature, humidity, smoke, water) and alarm events
UPS/backup power test logs and maintenance records for equipment protection
physical security inspection/audit reports and findings with remediation status
incident reports for physical breaches or equipment failures tied to facility identifiers
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: facility inventory cross-referenced with automated device/ sensor telemetry and access-control logs showing active controls; recent inspection/audit reports with remediation verified by timestamps.
Weak: only policy documents or one-off attestations without logs, missing device operational status, or manual checklists older than the last scheduled inspection.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control requires physical security to be 'designed and implemented', which is preventive by definition—establishing controls before incidents occur.

PES-74.differentiation_note: This control is focused on coverage and implementation of physical controls across tangible locations (offices/rooms/facilities) rather than on logical access, logging, or incident response; because the valid focus_area list lacks a pure 'physical_security' tag, nearby areas (access_control and cloud_and_infrastructure_security) were chosen and noted as a limitation.

PES-75.measurement_goal: Measure the number of unauthorized physical access attempts detected, the percentage of premises entry points continuously covered by monitoring systems, and the uptime/availability of those monitoring systems to prove continuous detection capability.
risk_categories: Access control, Physical and environmental security

metric_type_preferences.rationale: As a detective control the primary need is event counts (unauthorized access detections). Percentage and rate metrics are useful secondaries to express coverage and detections-per-time, while trends show detection frequency changes over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of detected unauthorized access events (alerts/alarms) per entry point per day, percent of designated entry points with active monitoring (camera/sensor/log feed) and monitoring-system uptime percentage, and mean time to detect/confirm unauthorized access from monitoring alerts. Also measure number of incidents escalated from monitoring events and retention duration of recorded evidence.
data_signals: video surveillance recording logs and timestamps (continuous recording availability)
door/access control event logs (badge swipes, door open/forced-entry events) with timestamps
alarm and intrusion detection events (forced-entry, motion sensors) with event IDs and timestamps
security monitoring system uptime/availability metrics (service/recorder health)
incident/ticket records created from monitoring alerts for unauthorized access
count of confirmed unauthorized access incidents vs total monitoring alerts
recording retention and deletion logs for surveillance footage
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: continuous automated recording and timestamped access-control logs correlated to alarm events with retention policies and high system uptime
Strong: low mean time to detect and documented incident tickets linking monitoring alert to response
Weak: periodic manual patrol logs or occasional screenshots without continuous timestamps or gaps in recording/coverage
Weak: self-reported annual checks or missing retention/uptime metrics

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control requires premises to be 'continuously monitored' for 'unauthorized physical access', which is a detection activity rather than prevention or correction.

PES-75.differentiation_note: This control specifically requires continuous detection of physical entry and monitoring-system availability (coverage and uptime), unlike sibling controls that might cover access provisioning, environmental controls, or backup/redundancy; measurement must therefore focus on live detection events, coverage percentage, and monitoring uptime rather than configuration or change metrics.

PES-76.measurement_goal: Measure coverage and operational effectiveness of physical and environmental protections across infrastructure: percentage of critical sites with required protections (fire suppression, UPS/generator, flood/water detection, environmental monitoring), count and rate of physical/environmental incidents (breaches, equipment failures) and MTTR for outages caused by physical or environmental events.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: As a corrective control the most useful primary metric is a rate (incident rate, remediation/restore rate, failure rate) to show how often physical/environmental protections fail or require corrective action; percentage (coverage) and count (incident/events) are complementary to show scope and volume.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: 1) Coverage: percentage of critical facilities/sites with implemented protections (fire suppression, UPS/generator redundancy, water/flood barriers, environmental sensors, physical access controls). 2) Operational effectiveness: count of physical/environmental incidents (breaches, equipment failures) and their root-cause classification, time-to-detect and time-to-restore (MTTR) per incident, and results of periodic tests (UPS/generator test runtimes, fire suppression tests, sensor calibration and alerts). 3) Maintenance/compliance: schedule adherence for maintenance and test records per asset.
data_signals: site inventory with critical asset classification and location
physical control implementation checklist per site (fire suppression, UPS/generator, water detection, flood barriers)
environmental sensor logs (temperature, humidity, water/leak detection) with timestamps and alert events
UPS/generator test records and runtimes with test timestamps
access control logs for secure areas and CCTV retention/inspection logs
incident tickets classified as 'physical' or 'environmental' with root-cause and resolution timestamps
maintenance schedule adherence records and completed maintenance logs
outage/downtime records attributed to equipment failure or environmental events
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: continuous sensor and access-control logs correlated with incident tickets and automated test records (UPS/generator runtimes, fire suppression tests) demonstrating both implementation and operational performance.
Strong: documented root-cause analyses linking incidents to physical/environmental causes and timely corrective actions (short MTTR).
Weak: only design documents or one-time site inspection reports without ongoing sensor logs, test records, or incident correlation.
Weak: manual checklists without timestamps or evidence of periodic testing and maintenance completion.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control metadata labels it 'type: corrective' and the description focuses on designing and implementing protections to respond to physical/environmental threats.

PES-76.differentiation_note: This control specifically targets design, implementation and operational evidence for physical and environmental protections (sensors, UPS, fire suppression, access controls) rather than logical access or IT patching; because the valid focus areas lack a dedicated 'physical_security' tag the taxonomy maps to infrastructure and data protection and notes that physical-specific sensor and maintenance evidence is the unique measurement need.

PES-77.measurement_goal: Measure the proportion of defined secure areas that have required security measures designed and implemented per policy and evidence the ongoing operational effectiveness (access control devices operational, CCTV coverage, visitor/escort controls, and remediation of physical security findings).
risk_categories: Access control, Physical and environmental security

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is a preventive control about design and implementation; auditors need coverage metrics (percentage of secure areas meeting policy) as primary evidence, with counts of exceptions, rates of unauthorized attempts, and checklist/audit scores as supplemental context.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Compute percentage of inventoried secure areas that have documented security measures and 'implemented' status (physical access controls installed and operational, CCTV coverage, documented visitor/escort procedures, and completed implementation checklists). Also capture counts of access-control exceptions, unauthorized access attempts, equipment failure incidents affecting secure areas, and remediation ticket completion rates.
data_signals: secure_area inventory with area identifier, classification, and policy-required controls
physical access control device inventory (badge readers, locks) with installation and operational status timestamps
access control event logs (badge-in/out, denied access, forced-open) with user/device id and timestamp
CCTV coverage map and camera uptime/availability logs
visitor sign-in/out logs and escort assignment records
physical security inspection and audit reports with findings and remediation ticket timestamps
equipment maintenance and failure logs for secure-area systems (power, locks, cameras)
policy document version and implementation checklist completion records
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated, continuous access logs and CCTV uptime correlated to an up-to-date secure-area inventory plus recurring inspection reports showing remediation closed within policy timeframes.
Weak: only a policy document or one-time manual checklist without device operational logs, or sporadic visitor registers with no correlation to access logs.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control requires security measures to be 'designed and implemented', which is inherently preventive (establishing controls to prevent breaches and unavailability).

PES-77.differentiation_note: This control focuses specifically on physical secure-area design and operational evidence (device inventories, CCTV, visitor/inspection logs) rather than digital access policy or incident detection; mapping to available focus areas is approximate because the valid list lacks an explicit 'physical_security' label.

PES-78.measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of workstations and shared desks that comply with the clear desk and clear screen policy (e.g., locked screens within policy timeout and no sensitive paper left on desks at end of day), plus the count and trend of observed policy violations detected by audits, CCTV or incident reports.
risk_categories: Access control, Physical and environmental security

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is a preventive, coverage-focused control: auditors need coverage percentages (e.g., percent of workstations configured and observed compliant). Counts and rates (violation counts, violations per audit) and trends over time are useful to show remediation and behavior change.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percent of workstations with screen-lock configured and observed active within the policy timeout, percent of desks found clear of sensitive documents during end-of-day or random clean-desk inspections, count of violations (unlocked screens, visible sensitive material) per audit cycle, and training completion/acknowledgement rates for the clear desk/screen policy.
data_signals: clean desk audit records (inspection timestamp, location, inspector, compliant: yes/no, notes)
screen lock event logs (session lock timestamps, user ID, workstation ID, lock timeout setting)
workstation configuration inventory (screen-lock timeout policy value per device)
CCTV or security camera snapshots/time-stamped images showing unattended devices or documents (where permitted)
incident/near-miss reports referencing unattended documents or unlocked screens
policy acknowledgement and training completion records for clear desk/screen
number of policy violations recorded per audit cycle and remediation action records
end-of-day checklist completions or badge-out workstation check logs
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated screen-lock event logs correlated with workstation inventory and regular, timestamped clean-desk audit reports showing high coverage; training completion records aligned with lower violation rates.
Moderate: periodic documented manual inspections without automated log correlation.
Weak: one-off attestations or anecdotal interview statements; isolated screenshots without audit chain-of-custody.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control is titled 'Clear desk and clear screen' and is intended to prevent unauthorized disclosure of information and reduce exposure from unattended devices/documents, which is classical preventive behavior control.

PES-78.differentiation_note: This control is focused on physical/behavioral prevention at the workstation/desk level (observable audits and device lock logs), unlike sibling controls that might address logical access controls or incident response — its measurements require direct observation data and workstation configuration records.

PES-79.measurement_goal: Measure the percent of company equipment that is located in authorized/secure sites and the percent that have required physical protections (locked cabinets, cages, environmental controls, backup power) in place; report the count of equipment items with missing or non-functional protections and the rate of physical protection failures or environmental alerts affecting equipment.
risk_categories: Access control, Physical and environmental security

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is a preventive control focused on coverage and configuration: auditors primarily need coverage percentages (what portion of equipment is 'sited securely' and 'protected'); counts and rates (non-compliant items, protection-failure events) are useful secondary measures and distribution helps show which sites or asset tiers are affected.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of assets in the authoritative asset inventory that are assigned to approved secure locations and have documented physical protections; count of assets missing required protections; timestamps of latest site assessments and protection maintenance; rate of environmental or physical-access incidents affecting protected equipment.
data_signals: asset inventory records with asset_id, physical_location, and criticality
site authorization/approval records (list of approved secure sites/data center IDs)
physical protection controls list per asset (rack locks, cages, UPS, fire suppression)
site assessment / inspection checklist results with timestamps and assessor
physical access control logs (badge entries) for rooms housing equipment
CCTV coverage mapping and footage retention logs for equipment locations
environmental monitoring alerts (temperature, humidity, water leak) correlated to equipment location
UPS/backup power test records and maintenance logs
recorded protection failures or breach incidents affecting equipment
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: an authoritative asset inventory keyed to secure-site IDs plus continuous environmental sensor logs and physical access-control events that are correlated to asset locations; up-to-date site assessment reports and maintenance test logs.
Weak: one-off photographs, ad-hoc spreadsheets without timestamps, or annual narrative statements that do not show ongoing protection, monitoring, or proof of remediation for identified gaps.
Strong: automated alerts and incident records showing remediation timelines vs policy; Weak: undocumented verbal approvals or undocumented exceptions.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The wording 'shall be sited securely and protected' indicates measures designed to prevent unauthorized access, damage, or unavailability, so the control is preventive.

PES-79.differentiation_note: This control specifically focuses on physical siting and protection of equipment (location, physical barriers, environmental controls) rather than logical access, logging, or incident response; measurement must emphasize asset-location mapping and physical-protection coverage. (Focus areas chosen are the closest matches from available identifiers; ISO language is inherently physical/environmental.)

PES-80.measurement_goal: Measure the availability, redundancy and tested operability of supporting utilities (power, environmental controls, backup generation/UPS and related maintenance) for critical systems: uptime percentage for utility services supporting critical assets, count of utility-related outages and failed tests, and mean time to restore utility service after failure.
risk_categories: Access control, Physical and environmental security

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This control is primarily preventive—auditors expect coverage and uptime percentages (e.g., utility uptime, percentage of critical systems with tested backup). Secondary metrics (counts of outages, rates of failed tests, trends in MTTR) show frequency and remediation performance.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Concrete metrics: (1) percentage uptime of supporting utilities (power/HVAC/backup) for systems classified as critical; (2) monthly count of utility-related incidents (power outages, HVAC failures) and failed utility tests; (3) time-to-recover (MTTR) for utility failures; (4) percentage of utility components with current successful test/maintenance records.
data_signals: power outage incident logs with timestamps and affected systems
UPS and backup generator test records (timestamps, pass/fail, duration)
environmental sensor readings and alert logs (temperature, humidity) correlated to system events
maintenance schedules and completion records for utility equipment (work orders)
redundancy/failover configuration documentation for utility systems (N+1, alternate feeds)
system availability metrics during utility events (service downtime windows)
incident root-cause analysis linking system outages to utility failures
asset criticality mapping showing which systems depend on each utility
evidence_strength_indicators: Continuous machine-generated logs from environmental sensors, UPS/generator monitoring and automated test records are strong evidence.
Regular scheduled test records with timestamps and pass/fail outcomes strengthen evidence over ad-hoc manual notes.
Point-in-time or single annual test reports, missing correlation between utility events and system outages, or lack of signed maintenance records are weak evidence.

affinity_keywords: unavailability of critical company data and systems

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control (supporting utilities) is intended to prevent system unavailability and equipment failures by ensuring utilities are available and resilient.

PES-80.differentiation_note: This control focuses on availability and resilience of physical utilities (power/HVAC/backup) supporting systems, not on access controls or project governance — measurement must emphasize utility uptime, tests and incident correlation; chosen focus areas are the closest matches from the provided list.

PES-81.measurement_goal: Measure the proportion of cable runs (power, data, supporting information services) that are physically protected (conduits/trays with covers, enclosed/locked pathways or rooms) and track counts of interception/interference/damage events and remedial actions per facility to demonstrate ongoing protection.
risk_categories: Access control, Physical and environmental security

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is a preventive control requiring coverage verification (percentage of cable runs protected). Counts of exposed/ damaged segments and trending of incidents are useful secondary metrics to show frequency and improvement over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Compute percent coverage = (number of cable segments documented as protected in conduit/tray/locked pathways) / (total number of cable segments carrying power or data). Also report count of exposed or unprotected cable segments, count and timestamps of interception/interference/damage incidents, and time since last physical inspection per site.
data_signals: cable inventory records (cable segment ID, type: power|data|supporting, route, protected_status)
physical inspection reports/checklists with timestamp and inspector ID (per site/segment)
CCTV footage or photo evidence of cable trays/conduits with timestamps
access control logs for comms rooms and cable-routing areas (entry timestamps, user IDs)
tamper-detection or cable-tray alarm events with timestamps
maintenance and incident tickets for cable damage/interception (timestamps, remediation actions)
network/service incident records linked to cable physical damage or interference
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: continuous/timestamped sources (inspection logs, CCTV/tamper alerts, up-to-date cable inventory showing protected_status for every segment) demonstrating full coverage.
Moderate: regular inspection reports combined with maintenance tickets but lacking continuous monitoring or complete inventory.
Weak: policy text or a one-time photo/inspection without an indexed inventory or follow-up incident records.

control_type_classification.reasoning: 'shall be protected' indicates preventive measures to stop interception, interference or damage before occurrence.

PES-81.differentiation_note: This control specifically focuses on physical protection and integrity of cable runs (coverage and tamper protection) rather than broader physical access controls or environmental systems; measurement must validate cable-level protection (inventory + inspection + tamper/CCTV evidence), so network/infrastructure signal types are emphasized — the chosen focus areas approximate physical domain since a dedicated 'physical_security' focus area is not in the valid list.

PRI-224.measurement_goal: Measure coverage and compliance of PII processing against legal, regulatory and contractual requirements: percent of PII processing activities and assets with a documented legal basis and mapping to applicable laws/contracts; percent of vendor and customer contracts containing required privacy clauses; training completion rate for personnel who handle PII; and count/rate of SARs/deletion requests met within required timeframes.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is a preventive, coverage-focused control so percentage metrics (coverage/compliance %) best demonstrate how fully requirements are met; counts and rates are useful secondary measures for absolute volumes (e.g., SAR counts, contracts missing clauses) and trends show change over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percent of PII processing activities and PII-holding assets documented in the PII/data inventory with an associated legal basis and mapped contractual obligations; percent of active customer/vendor contracts that include required privacy clauses; training completion percentage for privacy/PII handling; count and SLA compliance rate for subject access/deletion requests over the audit period.
data_signals: PII/data inventory records with processing purpose and legal basis fields
data processing activity (DPA) records and timestamps
consent records and consent timestamps where consent is the legal basis
contract register entries and contract clause versioning (indicator for privacy clauses/SCCs/BCRs)
international transfer mechanism records (SCCs, BCRs, documented legal mechanism)
vendor contract privacy control fields and contract review status
Article 27 appointment/registry record
DPIA report existence and completion dates
SAR and deletion request logs with received and closed timestamps and SLA status
privacy training assignment and completion logs per role
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: centralized, system-generated PII inventory with legal-basis and contract linkage, automated consent logs, contract register showing privacy clause flags, automated SAR tracking with SLA timestamps, and training LMS records showing role-based completions.
Weak: ad-hoc spreadsheets for inventory, emailed consents without timestamps, manual notes of contract clauses, or anecdotal training attestations without system records.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The description states 'identify and meet the requirements' and labels domain: PRIVACY and type: preventive, indicating this control is about establishing compliance coverage and preventing noncompliance.

PRI-224.differentiation_note: This control is high-level and focuses on legal/contractual coverage and recordkeeping for PII (legal basis, contracts, DPIAs, SARs and training), unlike operational controls that measure encryption or access enforcement—so measurements must combine inventory, contract, training and request-tracking signals rather than technical enforcement logs.

PRM-13.measurement_goal: Measure the proportion of ISMS-related projects and activities that have documented resource plans and assigned responsibilities, and quantify whether allocated resources (staffing/FTE and approved funding/commitments) meet the documented needs for establishment, implementation, maintenance and continual improvement of the ISMS.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is a preventive control about providing required resources; auditors need coverage and compliance proportions (percentage of projects/activities with resource plans and assigned responsibilities). Counts (number of unresourced projects, assigned roles) and trends (changes in provisioning over time) are useful secondary views.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of ISMS projects/activities with a documented resource plan and formal assignment of responsibilities; count of ISMS tasks with allocated FTEs or approved resource commitments; time-to-allocation (how long from project approval to resources provided). Measurements should tie resource records to specific ISMS lifecycle phases: establishment, implementation, maintenance, continual improvement.
data_signals: documented ISMS resource plans (project artefact with date, scope and required resources)
project plan entries referencing information security resources
role/responsibility assignment records (RACI entries, job descriptions, assignment dates)
staffing allocations or FTE records tied to ISMS activities
resource approval records or funding commitments for ISMS initiatives
count of ISMS projects/activities lacking assigned resources or responsibilities
time from ISMS project initiation to resource allocation
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated/project-management system records showing resource plans, assigned roles with start dates, and approval artifacts for funding; complete coverage across ISMS lifecycle phases.
Moderate: signed project charters and staffing tables stored in shared drives without automated linking to ISMS trackers.
Weak: informal meeting notes or ad-hoc emails referencing 'resources' without dated assignments or evidence of funding/allocated FTEs.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control requires the organization to 'determine and provide the resources' for ISMS activities, which is a proactive (preventive) action to avoid resourcing gaps.

PRM-13.differentiation_note: This control focuses on planning and allocation of resources for the ISMS lifecycle (establishment/maintenance/continual improvement) rather than technical or operational controls; measurement must track documented resource plans and assignments rather than technical indicators. (Focus area selection chosen as governance_risk_compliance/human_risk_and_training as closest matches.)

PRM-14.measurement_goal: Measure the proportion of projects that include documented information security activities (risk identification/assessment, security requirements, and security sign-off) and the count of projects missing assigned information security responsibilities; report trend of project-level security integration over time.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This preventive control requires coverage measurement (percentage of projects with security integrated). Counts of non-compliant projects and trend metrics over time are useful secondary measures; a compliance score can aggregate multiple project-level signals.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Compute percentage of active projects that have (a) a documented information security section in the project plan, (b) an attached project-level risk assessment, and (c) an assigned information security owner/sign-off. Also report count of projects lacking any of these items and average time between project initiation and security sign-off.
data_signals: project plan documents containing an 'information security' or 'security requirements' section (document ID, timestamp)
project-level risk assessment records attached to the project (record ID, creation timestamp)
project metadata field for assigned information security owner or role (owner id, role, assignment timestamp)
security review or approval records (approver id, approval timestamp, approval status)
issue-tracker tasks labeled 'security' linked to project (task id, status, remediation timestamps)
project onboarding/checklist entries showing 'security integrated' boolean and completion timestamp
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated project metadata fields and signed security approval records with timestamps and approver IDs; coverage shown across the full project registry.
Moderate: documented security sections in project plans stored in a central repository with timestamps but without approval records.
Weak: ad-hoc verbal attestations or one-off checklist notes without linkage to project IDs, timestamps, or approver identity.

control_type_classification.reasoning: Text states 'Information security shall be integrated into project management', indicating a requirement to prevent omission of security during projects (preventive).

PRM-14.differentiation_note: This control uniquely requires project-level coverage and role assignment evidence (project plans, risk assessments, assigned security owner) rather than system-level technical controls or operational detection metrics.

RSK-35.measurement_goal: Measure the proportion of identified risks and opportunities that have documented action plans integrated into ISMS processes, with assigned owners, due dates, and follow-up evaluations of effectiveness; and track the timeliness and closure rate of those actions to demonstrate continual improvement.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is a preventive planning control so percentage metrics (coverage/completion rates) best show how many risks have planned and integrated actions; counts and trends show volume and improvement over time; a score can summarize effectiveness evaluations.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Calculate the percentage of risks in the risk register that have a corresponding documented action plan with an assigned owner and due date, an integration status flag showing the action is embedded into ISMS/processes or project plans, and a completed effectiveness evaluation (post-implementation review). Also measure open vs closed counts, mean time from action creation to implementation, and trend of evaluated residual risk.
data_signals: risk register entries (risk id, description, source referencing clauses 4.1/4.2)
treatment/action records (action id, owner, assigned date, due_date, status open/closed, implementation timestamp)
integration traceability (links between action id and ISMS process or project plan item)
post-implementation effectiveness assessment reports or evaluation results (date, verdict, evidence)
management review minutes referencing planned actions and continual improvement outcomes
project plan artifacts showing security tasks included or missing (for project-related risks)
responsibility assignment records (RACI or role allocation logs)
mobile device risk treatment records (where applicable)
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: a continuously maintained risk register with timestamps, action objects linked to ISMS processes, completed effectiveness assessment reports, and management review minutes showing follow-up.
Moderate: periodic spreadsheets of actions with status updates but lacking traceability to ISMS processes or formal effectiveness evaluations.
Weak: one-off memos or informal notes of planned actions without assigned owners, dates, or evidence of integration and evaluation.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control directs planning and integration of actions to address risks and opportunities (preventing or reducing undesired effects), so it functions as a preventive governance control.

RSK-35.differentiation_note: This control is high-level and focuses on planning, integration, and evaluation of risk treatment actions across the ISMS (not just identification or monitoring), so measurements must capture traceability of actions into processes and effectiveness evaluations rather than only detection counts.

RSK-36.measurement_goal: Measure coverage and frequency of documented information security risk assessments, the count and distribution of identified risks by confidentiality/integrity/availability impact and likelihood, the share of risks with assigned owners, and the percentage of analysed risks prioritized and compared against established risk acceptance criteria for treatment.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: The control is detective and requires counting discrete assessment artifacts (assessments executed, risks identified, owners assigned) while also using percentages and distributions to show coverage and severity; trends show consistency and improvement across repeated assessments.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of risk assessment executions (date, scope, assessor) over the audit period; count and distribution of identified risks tagged with CIA impact, likelihood score, calculated risk level, and assigned risk owner; percentage of analysed risks that have a documented prioritization and an associated risk treatment plan. Also capture the presence and version of the documented risk criteria (including acceptance thresholds) and the assessment template/methodology used to demonstrate consistency.
data_signals: risk assessment execution records (assessment_id, date, scope, assessor_id)
documented risk criteria / risk acceptance criteria (version, effective_date)
risk register entries with fields: risk_id, description, confidentiality_impact, integrity_impact, availability_impact
likelihood_score and consequence/impact_score per risk
calculated risk level or rating per risk
risk_owner assignment field per risk record
risk treatment plan existence and treatment priority field
assessment template or methodology identifier used (to show consistent method)
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated/centralized risk register with timestamped assessment records, standardized scoring fields (likelihood, impact), explicit risk_owner, and linked treatment plans demonstrating consistent methodology across multiple assessment cycles.
Strong: retained documented risk criteria (acceptance thresholds) and versioned assessment templates used for each assessment.
Weak: ad-hoc spreadsheets or single point-in-time reports lacking scoring fields, missing owner assignments, or no evidence of comparison to formal risk acceptance criteria.

affinity_keywords: criteria for performing information security risk assessments
scope of the information security management system

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control requires identifying and analysing risks ('identify the information security risks', 'assess the potential consequences', 'assess the realistic likelihood'), which are detective assessment activities.

RSK-36.differentiation_note: This control focuses specifically on defining the risk assessment process, consistent application and documentation (criteria, scoring, owners, prioritization) rather than on treatment execution or monitoring of implemented controls; measurement must emphasize assessment artefacts, scoring fields and evidence of consistent methodology.

RSK-37.measurement_goal: Measure the number of completed risk treatment plans and Statements of Applicability (SOAs), the count and percentage of necessary controls mapped against Annex A, the count of controls marked as implemented vs. not implemented, and the count of risk owner approvals and documented residual risk acceptances.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Counts (number of SOAs, plans, approvals, excluded controls) directly demonstrate that the risk treatment process produced required artifacts; percentages show coverage (controls mapped/implemented) and trends show ongoing application and remediation over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of risk treatment plans produced and their approval timestamps; count of Statements of Applicability with full control lists, justification fields, and implemented/not-implemented flags; count of Annex A controls explicitly excluded with documented justification; and count of residual risk acceptance records signed by risk owners. Also measure the percentage of necessary controls that are implemented versus those planned or excluded.
data_signals: Statement of Applicability documents including control list, justification text, and implemented/not-implemented flag
risk treatment plan records with risk owner approval signatures/timestamps
mapping matrix or spreadsheet linking selected controls to Annex A control identifiers
records of excluded Annex A controls with documented justification for exclusion
residual risk acceptance records or signed acknowledgements from risk owners
documented information metadata (creation date, last modified, version, document owner)
implementation status change timestamps for controls (when a control moved to 'implemented')
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: machine-readable SOA and mapping matrix showing each necessary control mapped to Annex A with justification, implemented flag, and signed timestamped approvals stored in a version-controlled repository.
Moderate: PDF SOAs and plans with visible signatures and dates but no machine-readable mapping to Annex A.
Weak: single undated/manual documents or verbal attestations without approvals, no mapping to Annex A, or missing residual risk acceptance records.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control requires producing and retaining artifacts (SOA, treatment plans, approvals) to verify selection and implementation of controls, which is consistent with a detective verification activity.

RSK-37.differentiation_note: This control is specifically about producing/mapping risk treatment artifacts and obtaining approvals (SOA, treatment plan, residual risk acceptance) rather than executing technical controls or responding to incidents; measurements must focus on documentation completeness, mapping to Annex A, and approval evidence.

RSK-38.measurement_goal: Measure the number and timing of information security risk assessments executed versus the planned schedule, the proportion of projects/changes that had a risk assessment performed when significant changes occurred, and the presence and retention metadata of documented assessment results.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Counts of assessments and assessments linked to change events directly show whether assessments are being performed (detective control). Percentages measure coverage (projects/changes assessed) and trends show recurring compliance with planned intervals over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of risk assessment executions (with timestamps) mapped to a published risk assessment schedule; count and percentage of projects or significant change requests that include a linked risk assessment report; retention metadata (creation, last modified, retention period) for stored assessment documents; timeliness metric for assessments performed within the planned interval or within a defined window after a change.
data_signals: risk assessment execution records with timestamp and assessor identifier
official risk assessment schedule / planned intervals (dates and frequency)
risk assessment report/document links or IDs stored in document repository
change requests or significant-change records indicating linkage to a risk assessment
project plan records with attached or referenced risk assessment
document retention metadata (created_at, last_modified, retention_policy_tag)
coverage matrix mapping assets/projects to last risk assessment date
risk treatment/action item tracker entries created from assessment results
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: system-generated assessment records with timestamps, automated linkage between change control entries and assessment reports, and a coverage matrix showing per-project/asset last assessment dates.
Weak: ad-hoc undated Word/PDF files, manual attestations without timestamps, or inconsistent storage locations that prevent demonstrating scheduled or change-triggered assessments.

affinity_keywords: results of the information security risk assessments

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control requires performing risk assessments and retaining results (identification and detection of risk), matching detective behavior: 'perform information security risk assessments at planned intervals or when significant changes ...'.

RSK-38.differentiation_note: This control specifically requires scheduled and change-triggered risk assessments and documented retention of results, unlike sibling controls that may focus on treatment or remediation — measurement must link assessment records to the schedule and to change/project records.

RSK-39.measurement_goal: Measure the proportion and count of identified information security risks that have documented, approved treatment plans with assigned owners and completed mitigation actions within defined timeframes; track residual risk ratings and inclusion of security risk treatment in project plans and remote-work-site mitigations.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Risk treatment is corrective in nature (implementing mitigations) so rates (treatment completion rate, remediation velocity) are primary; percentage coverage (percent of risks with plans) and counts (open/overdue risks) provide complementary visibility and trends show improvement over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of risks recorded in the risk register by risk id and criticality, boolean field indicating existence of an approved treatment plan, assigned owner id, treatment plan approval timestamp, treatment implementation start and completion timestamps, residual risk rating after treatment, and linkage of risk entries to project plan IDs or remote site assessment records. Measure percent of risks with implemented treatment within policy SLA and number of risks without assigned owners.
data_signals: risk register entries (risk_id, description, initial_risk_rating, asset_scope)
treatment_plan_present flag and treatment_plan_approval_timestamp per risk_id
treatment_owner (user id) assigned to each risk
treatment_implementation_start_timestamp and treatment_implementation_completion_timestamp
residual_risk_rating or residual_risk_level after treatment
project_plan documents or project_plan_id references linking to risk_id (evidence that project plans address the risk)
remote_work_site_assessment reports or mitigation records linked to risk_id
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: machine-readable risk register with timestamps, owners, approved treatment plan documents, and implementation evidence (change tickets, config changes, assessment reports) linked to risk IDs; automated linkage between project plans and risk entries.
Weak: ad-hoc spreadsheets or email approvals without timestamps/owners, narrative notes without implementation artifacts, or annual-only reports that don't show ongoing treatment progress.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The term 'risk treatment' implies implementing mitigations and reducing risk (corrective actions) such as assigning responsibility and applying controls.

RSK-39.differentiation_note: This control focuses specifically on treatment (existence, assignment, implementation, and residual risk) and on ensuring treatments are integrated into project plans and remote-work-site mitigations — measurement must capture treatment plan lifecycle and project linkage rather than just identification.

SAT-44.measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of personnel and relevant interested parties who have been assigned and completed role-specific information security awareness, education and training within the required period; measure the percentage who have acknowledged receipt of regular updates to the organization's information security policy, topic-specific policies and procedures; and track assessment pass-rates and overdue counts by job function.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is a preventive control requiring coverage and completion (best expressed as percentages of population and acknowledgement rates). Counts of completions and overdue items, rates of assignment/acknowledgement, and trends over time are useful secondary measures to show coverage and improvement.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of employees and listed relevant interested parties with an active role mapping who have (a) been assigned required information security training, (b) completed required training within the mandated interval, and (c) acknowledged receipt of the organization's information security policy and topic-specific procedure updates; include assessment scores and counts of overdue or missed training by job function.
data_signals: LMS training assignment records with job function/role mapping
training completion timestamps and completion status per user
policy update distribution logs and policy version identifiers
policy acknowledgement timestamps (signed/emailed/recorded) per recipient
training assessment scores and pass/fail status
overdue training notifications and current overdue counts by role
training refresh schedule and last-delivery timestamps per module
directory/list of relevant interested parties and contact assignments
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated LMS logs showing assignment → completion timestamps and assessment scores (strong);
Policy distribution records with recorded acknowledgements soon after policy updates (strong);
Manual attestation or one-off emailed training records without timestamps or role mapping (weak);
Quarterly or continuous trend of completion rates and declining overdue counts indicates strong sustained control; single point-in-time spreadsheets are weaker.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control requires personnel to 'receive appropriate information security awareness, education and training and regular updates', which is focused on preventing security lapses through training and policy distribution.

SAT-44.differentiation_note: This control specifically requires role/job-function aligned training and recorded acknowledgements of policy updates for both personnel and relevant interested parties, unlike sibling controls that might focus solely on incident handling or technical controls; measurement must therefore include role mapping, policy-distribution evidence and assessment results.

SEA-54.measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of hosts and network devices whose clocks are within the organization-defined time offset tolerance, the count of devices out-of-sync, and the time-to-remediate for out-of-sync devices to demonstrate consistent clock synchronization and reliable timestamps for logs and operations.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Clock sync is primarily a coverage/quality control problem (what proportion of assets meet the tolerated offset) so percentage is most useful; counts of out-of-sync devices and trending of offset over time are useful secondary measures to drive remediation and detect degradation.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: A queryable inventory that returns, per asset: configured time source(s), last successful sync timestamp, current clock offset in seconds relative to authoritative time source, number of failed sync attempts within the observation window, and remediation/repair timestamps when offsets were corrected. Aggregate these to compute percent-in-tolerance, count of out-of-tolerance devices, and mean time-to-correct.
data_signals: per-host last NTP/chrony sync timestamp
per-host current clock offset (seconds) relative to reference time
configured time server(s) list per device (NTP/chrony/Windows time)
number of failed time-sync attempts or sync errors per host in last 24/72 hours
time-source reachability checks (ICMP/TCP) to authoritative time servers
timestamp drift distribution across hosts (max/min/median offset)
configuration-management records showing time-sync settings applied (playbook/agent audit)
remediation/ticket timestamps for corrected out-of-sync devices
evidence_strength_indicators: Continuous per-host telemetry of offset seconds and automated sync logs (strong) vs. single manual snapshots or screenshots (weak)
Centralized time-source logs correlated with per-host offsets (strong) vs. inconsistent local host reports with no authoritative reference (weak)
Recorded remediation tickets and timestamps showing correction within organizational SLA (strong) vs. undocumented manual fixes (weak)

control_type_classification.reasoning: Clock synchronization prevents operational and forensic issues by keeping system clocks within tolerance, implied by the control name 'Clock synchronization' which enforces correct time rather than detecting incidents after they occur.

SEA-54.differentiation_note: Unlike controls that focus on access or responsibility assignment, this control specifically requires measurement of time offsets, authoritative time-source configuration, and continuous sync telemetry (i.e., precision of timestamps) rather than policy or role coverage.

SEA-55.measurement_goal: Measure the proportion and count of application development and acquisition projects that have documented, specified information security requirements with recorded approval/sign-off and traceability to design/test artifacts; surface outstanding projects or acquisitions missing approved requirements and any approved exceptions.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is a preventive control requiring coverage and approval of requirements: percentage (coverage of projects with approved security requirements) is the most direct auditor metric; counts (number of projects missing requirements) and scores (completeness/traceability score) provide complementary detail.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Compute percentage = (number of active development/acquisition projects with documented and approved security requirements) / (total active development/acquisition projects). Also provide counts of projects with no security requirements, number of approved exceptions/waivers, timestamps of requirement approval, and traceability links from each requirement to design/test artifacts.
data_signals: security_requirement_specification_documents (per project or acquisition)
requirement_approval_records (approval_signatures_or_approval_timestamps)
project_initiation_document / project_plan security requirement section presence flag
acquisition/procurement documentation showing security clauses for acquired applications
requirements_traceability_matrix entries linking requirements → design/test cases
exceptions_or_waiver_records for missing requirements
assigned_security_responsibility records (role/owner per project)
security_requirement_version_history and change timestamps
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated/centralized registry of requirement documents with approval timestamps and traceability matrix entries for each active project (demonstrates systematic coverage).
Moderate: sampled project folders containing signed requirement documents and procurement clauses (demonstrates spot compliance but misses coverage).
Weak: single example project or verbal attestation without traceability or approval records (insufficient to prove control operates for all projects).

control_type_classification.reasoning: Language 'shall be identified, specified and approved when developing or acquiring applications' indicates a preventive control to stop insecure apps by requiring requirements and approval up-front.

SEA-55.differentiation_note: This control specifically requires documented and approved security requirements tied to development/acquisition activities (coverage and approval evidence) rather than runtime controls or vulnerability remediation metrics; measurement must focus on requirement documents, approvals and traceability rather than scans or incident data.

SEA-56.measurement_goal: Measure the rate and coverage of secure system architecture activities: percentage of projects with documented architecture/security design reviews and threat models completed before implementation, count of design-related findings (e.g., insecure design, missing security requirements) per project, and remediation completion rate for design findings within defined policy timeframes.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: SEA-56 is a preventive, design-phase control so percentage metrics (coverage/compliance rate) show how widely secure architecture practices are applied; counts of findings and trend over time indicate effectiveness and improvement.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: For each project or system change, capture a binary/compliant flag for 'architecture/security review completed' with timestamp, number of design findings discovered (tagged by type/severity), remediation actions and completion timestamps, presence of security requirements in project plans, and assignment of security responsibilities. Aggregate into % of projects with completed reviews, open finding counts by severity, and % remedied within policy SLA.
data_signals: architecture review completion records with project identifiers and timestamps
threat model artifacts or design review reports linked to project IDs
security requirements/status field in project plans (presence/absent)
design finding tickets with severity, owner, creation and resolution timestamps
remediation action records and completion timestamps
assigned information security role/responsibility records for each project
remote work / endpoint configuration compliance reports tied to system design
incident/breach records attributed to design/architecture faults
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: per-project automated records (architecture review artifacts, threat models) and tracked remediation tickets showing timestamps and closure within SLA.
Strong: integration of architecture checks into project lifecycle tools (CI/CD gates, project templates) with full traceability.
Weak: verbal attestations, one-off summary reports, or absence of timestamps and owners for findings.
Weak: only retrospective incident reports without design-time artifacts linking prevention activities to projects.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control focuses on architecture and engineering principles applied during design to prevent security issues (design-time controls), so it is primarily preventive.

SEA-56.differentiation_note: This control focuses on design-phase secure architecture and engineering practices (project-level reviews, threat models, assigned responsibilities) rather than operational detection or incident response; measurement must track per-project artifacts and remediation of design findings. (Control description was not provided; taxonomy leverages associated risk text.)

TDA-37.measurement_goal: Measure the proportion of source code repositories, development tools, and software library endpoints that enforce least-privilege read/write access controls, and track counts of privileged (write) accounts, orphaned accounts, and approved access changes over time to demonstrate appropriate management.
risk_categories: Communications security, Software development and acquisition

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is a preventive control focused on coverage and configuration (primary: percentage of repositories/tools with appropriate read/write controls). Counts of privileged accounts and trends over time are useful secondary metrics to show scale and improvement or regression.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of source code repositories, development tools, and software library endpoints with documented least-privilege read/write permissions and access controls enabled; counts of accounts with write privileges per repository/tool; number of access grant/change requests with recorded approvals and timestamps; number of orphaned or inactive accounts with repository access.
data_signals: repository and package-repository access control lists (ACLs) showing read/write permissions per user/group
list of development tools (IDs) and their configured access controls
software library/package manager access configurations
record of access provisioning/change requests with approver and timestamp
access review results and review timestamps for repositories/tools
count of accounts with write (push/merge) privileges per repository
orphaned/inactive account list that still has repository/tool access
audit logs of repository commits/operations with actor identifier and timestamps (to correlate access usage)
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated inventory of repositories/tools with ACL exports and continuous mapping to IAM identities plus signed access-approval records and recurring access review outcomes
Strong: MFA/enforcement and provisioning workflow logs proving policy enforcement for write access
Weak: one-time manual spreadsheets, screenshots, or verbal attestations without linkage to live ACLs or approval records
Weak: absence of timestamps or approver identity on access changes

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control text explicitly includes 'type: preventive' and mandates that read/write access 'shall be appropriately managed', indicating preventive access controls and configuration requirements.

TDA-37.differentiation_note: Unlike sibling controls that might target vulnerability scanning or runtime detection, this control specifically requires measuring access configuration and provisioning for source code, developer tools, and libraries—so measurements must map to repository/tool ACLs, provisioning logs, and access-review records rather than vulnerability counts or incident alerts.

TDA-38.measurement_goal: Demonstrate that an approved secure development policy exists and is applied across active software/systems projects by measuring policy existence (document/version), proportion of projects that have completed required SDLC security checklists/gates, and the count and justification of any exceptions or failures to apply the rules.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is a preventive control requiring coverage and application of rules across projects; percentage metrics (coverage/compliance %) directly show how widely the SDLC rules are applied, while counts and rates capture exceptions, gate failures, and remediation velocity and trends over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: 1) Existence: the approved SDLC/secure development policy document id and approval timestamp. 2) Application: percentage of active development projects/repositories with a completed secure-development checklist and passing CI/CD security gates before release. 3) Exceptions: count of documented waivers/approved exceptions and timestamps. 4) Technical enforcement where applicable: number of dev environments with encryption-in-transit enabled and segregation-of-duties assignments for build/release roles.
data_signals: SDLC / secure development policy document ID and approval timestamp
inventory of active development projects and repositories with a SDLC-compliance flag
secure-development checklist completion records per project (checklist item IDs, completed_by, completed_at)
CI/CD pipeline security gate logs with pass/fail status and timestamps
recorded exceptions/waivers for SDLC rules with approver and justification
vulnerability findings tagged as 'introduced-in-development' with discovery timestamps
encryption-in-transit configuration audit results for development environments
role assignment records showing segregation of duties for development/build/release roles
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated CI/CD gate logs showing per-release pass/fail results and timestamps, project-level checklist records tied to repository IDs, and a current mapping of all active projects to a policy-compliance flag.
Weak: only a signed policy document with no project-level compliance records, or annual attestations without pipeline/enforcement logs.
Strong: configuration scans proving encryption-in-transit in dev environments and automated role assignment records; Weak: manual developer assertions or inconsistent spreadsheet lists of projects.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control mandates that 'Rules ... shall be established and applied', indicating preventive controls focused on policy establishment and enforcement before incidents occur.

TDA-38.differentiation_note: This control is about establishment and application of SDLC rules (policy coverage and enforcement) rather than technical detection or remediation metrics — measurement must focus on policy existence, project-level coverage and enforcement logs rather than just vulnerability counts or incident response records.

TDA-39.measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of code artifacts and development workflows that enforce secure coding principles (policy adoption), count of secure-coding violations detected per development cycle, and the rate at which violations are remediated or blocked by CI gates.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: As a preventive control, auditors need coverage/adhesion metrics (percentage of repos/PRs covered by secure-coding checks). Counts of violations and trends over time complement coverage to show scale and improvement.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of code repositories and pull requests with automated secure-coding checks enabled and passing; count of distinct secure-coding rule violations discovered by CI (SAST/lint) per sprint; remediation or blocking rate (how many PRs were blocked or fixed before merge). Also track developer secure-coding training completion tied to active committers.
data_signals: SAST scan results with rule IDs, file path, commit/PR id, and timestamp
static/linting rule violation counts per repository and per PR
CI pipeline policy results indicating pass/fail for security gates
number and IDs of repositories with SAST/linter enabled (coverage list)
pull request metadata showing security-related comments and remediation commits
time-to-remediate security violations (remediation timestamps vs detection timestamps)
developer secure-coding training completion records mapped to committer IDs
secure coding policy document version and date of adoption per project
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated SAST/linter results integrated into CI that block merges when security rules fail and high repository coverage; evidence ties violations to specific PRs/commits and shows remediation timestamps.
Moderate: regular automated scans with non-blocking reporting plus documented code-review checklists referencing secure coding rules.
Weak: occasional manual checklists, ad-hoc post-release findings, or solely training records without telemetry showing enforcement in CI.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The text mandates applying secure coding principles to development, which is a preventive measure to stop vulnerabilities being introduced.

TDA-39.differentiation_note: This control targets developer-facing, code-level prevention (policy + tooling in CI) rather than runtime detection or patch management—measurements must focus on code repositories, CI enforcement, and developer training rather than production logs.

TDA-40.measurement_goal: Measure the proportion of development components and build pipelines that have defined and implemented security testing (SAST/DAST/SCA or equivalent) in the development lifecycle, the execution rate and pass-rate of those tests per build/PR, and the rate at which security test failures are triaged and remediated according to project policy.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: As a preventive control, evidence focuses on coverage and configuration (percentage of repos/components with security tests defined and CI-integrated). Counts (test runs, failures) and trends (execution/pass-rate over time) supplement coverage to show ongoing implementation.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Compute: (A) % of active code repositories or projects with CI/CD pipelines that include one or more automated security test stages (SAST/DAST/SCA) defined; (B) count and pass-rate of security test executions per build/PR over a rolling period; and (C) mean time from a failed security test result to creation of a remediation ticket and to closure.
data_signals: CI/CD pipeline configuration files indicating security test stages (job names, tooling references)
SAST scan execution logs and results keyed by commit/PR with timestamps
DAST/acceptance environment test run logs and results with timestamps
SCA / dependency scan results per build
build/pr status for security test jobs (pass/fail) per commit
repository inventory mapped to projects and criticality
issue tracker tickets created from security test failures with timestamps and resolution status
traceability artifacts or test plans showing security tests defined in the development lifecycle
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated CI/CD pipeline configs stored as code plus continuous execution logs showing per-commit security test runs and remediation tickets tracked in the issue tracker.
Strong: coverage metrics showing high % of repos with defined security test stages and high PR gate pass-rate.
Weak: one-off manual penetration test reports, ad-hoc acceptance tests without CI integration, or undocumented test plans.

control_type_classification.reasoning: Control text mandates that 'Security testing processes shall be defined and implemented' in the development lifecycle — this is a preventive requirement to stop insecure code entering environments.

TDA-40.differentiation_note: This control is specifically about defining and implementing security testing within the development and acceptance phases (process and CI integration coverage) rather than detective metrics (e.g., incident counts) or remediation performance alone; measurement emphasis is on coverage/configuration and continuous test execution visibility.

TDA-41.measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of systems and applications whose environment (development, test, production) is correctly classified and isolated per policy, plus counts of cross-environment access events and instances of production data present in non-production environments and outstanding segregation exceptions.
risk_categories: Communications security, Software development and acquisition

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is a preventive control requiring coverage and compliance (best expressed as % of environments properly separated). Counts of cross-environment access events and trends over time are useful secondary measures to show exceptions and changes.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Calculate: (1) percentage of assets and applications tagged/classified as dev/test/prod and deployed in isolated network/tenant segments as required; (2) count of cross-environment access events (e.g., dev→prod access) over the audit period; (3) count of non-production datasets containing production data or lacking masking; and (4) number and age of approved segregation exceptions.
data_signals: environment inventory with environment classification tags (development/testing/production)
network segmentation configs (VPC/subnet/tenant mapping) showing environment boundaries
firewall/security-group/access-control rules that permit cross-environment traffic
IAM role/policy attachments and cross-environment permissions (who can access prod from non-prod)
CI/CD pipeline deployment configuration and approvals (pipelines restricted by environment)
data copy/sanitization logs showing production data copied to non-production and masking status
access/authentication logs showing cross-environment access events with timestamps and principals
record of approved segregation exceptions with issue date, approver, and expiration
